results.title_inferred: Bai Xian Pi (White Fresh Bark) — Dictamnus Root Bark for Damp-Heat and Skin Disease
results.original_text: 白鮮皮 — 味苦，寒。主頭風，黃疸，咳逆，淋瀝，女子陰中腫痛，濕痺死肌，不可屈伸起止行步。
results.translation:
Bai Xian Pi [White Fresh Bark; 白鮮皮]. Flavor: bitter [ku]. Nature: cold [han]. Primary actions: governs wind in the head [tou feng], jaundice [huang dan], cough with counterflow [ke ni], urinary dribbling and obstruction [lin li], swelling and pain within the female genitalia [nü zi yin zhong zhong tong], damp-[type] impediment [shi bi] with dead or numb flesh [si ji], and inability to flex, extend, rise, stop, or walk.

[Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c.200 CE] Bai Xian Pi is classed among the middle-grade herbs [zhong pin] in the classical hierarchy — substances with some toxicity or marked action, used therapeutically under appropriate conditions rather than for prolonged tonic use.

[Channel Tropism — gui jing, per later elaboration] Enters the Spleen [Pi], Stomach [Wei], and Bladder [Pangguang] channels. Some authorities, including the Jin-Yuan and Ming commentators, additionally assign it to the Large Intestine channel.

[Five Flavors — wu wei] Bitter [ku].

[Four Natures — si qi] Cold [han].

[Classical therapeutic actions, synthesized from Shennong Bencaojing and subsequent Kampo / Bencao elaborations]
1. Clears heat and resolves toxins [qing re jie du]: indicated for heat-toxin accumulations, abscesses, and inflamed sores.
2. Dispels damp-heat [qu shi re]: the primary modern indication; used for skin diseases characterized by weeping, itching, redness, and crusting — including wind-rash [feng zhen], tinea [xuan], and eczema [shi zhen] — where the pathomechanism is damp-heat lurking in the skin and flesh.
3. Dispels wind and stops itching [qu feng zhi yang]: addresses the wind component of damp-wind-heat skin disorders.
4. Clears damp-heat from the Lower Burner [xia jiao shi re]: treats jaundice [huang dan] of the damp-heat type, urinary difficulty and dribbling [lin zheng], and genital swelling or itching in women.
5. Unblocks impediment [tong bi]: for damp-heat bi-syndrome presenting with joint heaviness, pain, and restricted movement.

[Japanese Kampo name] Byakusenpi [白鮮皮 — identical characters, Japanese on-reading]. Kampo pharmacopoeial sources (e.g., the Wakan Sansai Zue and later Meiji-period materia medica) preserve the classical Shennong indications and add emphasis on its role in heat-type skin eruptions [hifukka ryōiki], particularly in formulas for urticaria and chronic eczema. Kampo practitioners commonly pair it with Ku Shen [苦參, Sophora root] for intensely itching, weeping lesions.

[Key classical and Kampo formulas]
• Bai Xian Pi Tang [白鮮皮湯]: clears damp-heat, dispels wind, stops itching; used for wind-rash and skin sores.
• Xiao Feng San [消風散]: a Ming-dynasty formula (from the Wai Ke Zheng Zong) for wind-damp-heat skin disease; some versions incorporate Bai Xian Pi.
• Ku Shen Wan / Ku Shen Tang compound preparations: Bai Xian Pi is frequently combined with Ku Shen [苦參] and Di Fu Zi [地膚子] for damp-heat genital itching and skin lesions.
• In Kampo, Onsei-in [温清飲] and related formulas are sometimes modified with Byakusenpi for chronic, oozing dermatoses.

[Preparation methods, classical and Kampo]
— Decoction [tang]: 6–15 g dried root bark, decocted in water; standard internal use.
— Topical wash [xun xi / xi fang]: decocted liquid applied externally to affected skin areas for itching, tinea, and eczema; a common adjunct in both Chinese and Kampo practice.
— Powdered form [san]: ground and incorporated into pills or applied topically with sesame oil as a paste for localized skin lesions.

[Contraindications, per classical sources] Use with caution in patients with deficiency-cold of the Spleen and Stomach [pi wei xu han]; the herb's bitter-cold nature can injure Spleen yang with prolonged use or in constitutionally weak patients.

entities_mentioned.name: Bai Xian Pi 白鮮皮
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dictamnus dasycarpus Turcz. — root bark (family Rutaceae); commonly called White Dittany Root Bark or Gas Plant root bark in Western botanical literature
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification of 白鮮皮 with Dictamnus dasycarpus is consistent across Chinese pharmacopoeial sources (Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020, Vol. I), Japanese Kampo pharmacopoeia, and Western sinobotanical scholarship. The plant's distinctive bitter alkaloids (including dictamnine, obacunone, and fraxinellone) provide a plausible phytochemical basis for its classical anti-inflammatory, antipruritic, and antimicrobial actions.

entities_mentioned.name: Ku Shen 苦參
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sophora flavescens Aiton — root (family Fabaceae/Leguminosae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently paired with Bai Xian Pi in formulas targeting damp-heat skin disease and genital itching; shares bitter flavor and cold nature.

entities_mentioned.name: Di Fu Zi 地膚子
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kochia scoparia (L.) Schrad. — mature fruit (family Chenopodiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A common third ingredient in compound formulas with Bai Xian Pi and Ku Shen for urinary and skin damp-heat conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Xiao Feng San 消風散
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Kampo / TCM formula from Wai Ke Zheng Zong (外科正宗, 1617 CE, Chen Shigong). Treats wind-damp-heat skin eruptions. Not a plant entity but a compound formula; listed here for formulaic context.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source stub provided minimal primary-text content ('damp heat, skin disease') alongside the plant identification. The original_text field reconstructs the core Shennong Bencaojing entry for Bai Xian Pi from the standard received text tradition (e.g., as preserved in the Sun Xingyan / Sun Penshan 1799 critical recension and the Mori Tateyuki Shennong Honzō Kyō commentary), which is the appropriate classical source for this corpus. Passages not directly from the Shennong Bencaojing are marked with bracketed attributions.
2. The Japanese Kampo name Byakusenpi [白鮮皮] is a direct sinoxenic reading; there is no divergent Japanese-coined name for this drug, as it entered Kampo directly from Tang and Song Chinese bencao literature.
3. Classical herb grading: placement in 'middle-grade' [zhong pin] is consistent with the received Shennong Bencaojing classification; some later editors place it in the lower grade [xia pin] given its strong bitter-cold properties, but the middle-grade assignment is the majority-tradition reading.
4. Channel tropism assignments are post-Shennong elaborations (Jin-Yuan period onward) and are not original to the c.200 CE text; they are included per the entry schema's requirement for comprehensive Kampo elaboration coverage.
5. No genuinely illegible passages in source; minimal source stub was expanded from established classical scholarship rather than transcribed from a damaged document.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Bai Xian Pi 白鮮皮

results.title_inferred: Di Fu Zi (地膚子): Kochia Fruit — Damp-Heat, Urinary Disorders, and Skin Itching
results.original_text: 地膚子，味苦，寒。主膀胱熱，利小便，補中益精氣。久服，耳目聰明，輕身耐老。一名地葵。生平澤及田野。

entities_mentioned.name: Di Fu Zi 地膚子
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kochia scoparia (L.) Schrad. — mature fruit/seed; family Chenopodiaceae (now Amaranthaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The plant is a common annual herb widespread across temperate East Asia. The medicinal part is the mature fruit (technically a utricle enclosing the seed), harvested in autumn. The alternate classical name Di Kui (地葵) refers to its resemblance to mallow. Modern pharmacological studies confirm diuretic, antipruritic, and antimicrobial properties broadly consistent with classical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Di Kui 地葵
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternate classical name for Kochia scoparia fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonym appearing in the Shennong Bencaojing text; 'Ground Mallow,' reflecting a morphological comparison to mallow (Malva) leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Jifushi じふし (地膚子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kochia scoparia fruit, as used in Japanese Kampo medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: The Kampo tradition adopted the herb largely intact from the Chinese classical canon, preserving its primary indications for damp-heat lin syndrome and pruritic skin conditions. It appears in Kampo dermatology protocols.

entities_mentioned.name: Lin syndrome 淋證
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Broadly corresponds to urinary tract irritation syndromes: UTI, urethritis, cystitis, urolithiasis
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical lin syndrome encompasses several subtypes (shi lin / stone, re lin / heat, xue lin / blood, gao lin / turbid, qi lin / qi) — Di Fu Zi is most applicable to re lin (heat-type), characterized by burning, scanty, dark urine.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a modern descriptive label rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage; accordingly, the original_text field has been restored to the standard received text of the Shennong Bencaojing entry for Di Fu Zi as preserved in the Zhenghe Bencao and Bencao Gangmu traditions. The translation and therapeutic profile integrate: (1) the Shennong Bencaojing core text (c.200 CE); (2) elaborations in the Mingyi Bielu (名醫別錄, Tao Hongjing, c.500 CE); (3) Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596); and (4) Kampo clinical tradition. Channel tropism (gui jing) to Bladder and Kidney is a post-Han systematization not explicit in the original Shennong text but standard in all major later commentaries. The long-term tonic claims (sharpened senses, anti-aging) are characteristic Shennong superior-herb rhetoric and should be read in that formulaic context rather than as clinical claims.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Di Fu Zi 地膚子

{
  "title_inferred": "Che Qian Zi (Plantain Seed) — Classical Materia Medica Entry",
  "original_text": "車前子 — 味甘，寒。主氣癃，止痛，利水道小便，除濕痹。久服輕身耐老。一名當道。生平澤及道旁。",
  "translation": "Che Qian Zi (車前子, Plantain Seed)

Chinese Name: Chē Qián Zǐ (車前子)
Japanese Kampo Name: Shazenshi (シャゼンシ / 車前子)
Classical Category: Superior Herb (上品, shàng pǐn) — suitable for prolonged use without harm.

Five Flavors [Wǔ Wèi 五味]: Sweet (甘, gān); some later commentators also note a mild saltiness.
Four Natures [Sì Qì 四氣]: Cold (寒, hán).
Channel Tropism [Guī Jīng 歸經]: Kidney (腎), Liver (肝), Lung (肺), Small Intestine (小腸).

--- Shennong Bencaojing (c.200 CE) Core Text ---
'Flavor: sweet; nature: cold. Governs [disorders of] qi blockage [氣癃, qì lóng — urinary obstruction with qi stagnation]; arrests pain; promotes flow through the water passages [水道] and urination; eliminates damp impediment [濕痹, shī bì — painful obstruction due to dampness]. Prolonged consumption lightens the body and resists aging. Alternate name: Dāng Dào (當道, "grows in the road"). Grows in marshes and alongside roads.'

--- Expanded Classical and Kampo Therapeutic Actions ---
1. PROMOTES URINATION AND DRAINS DAMPNESS (利水滲濕, lì shuǐ shèn shī): Principal action. Treats dysuria, urinary obstruction, edema, and lin syndrome [淋證 — painful dribbling urination], particularly damp-heat patterns in the lower burner. Unusual property noted in classical texts: clears heat and promotes urination without depleting body fluids.
2. CLEARS HEAT AND BRIGHTENS THE EYES (清熱明目, qīng rè míng mù): Addresses liver-heat or liver-kidney deficiency patterns presenting with red, painful, or dry eyes, photophobia, and dimming of vision (目赤腫痛, 目暗昏花). This action was significantly elaborated in post-Han texts and is central to Kampo use.
3. RESOLVES PHLEGM AND CLEARS LUNG HEAT (清肺化痰, qīng fèi huà tán): Treats cough with yellow phlegm associated with lung heat. Elaborated in Tang and Song dynasty commentaries and adopted in Kampo practice.
4
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Che Qian Zi 車前子

results.title_inferred: Yi Yi Ren (Coix Seed): Classical and Kampo Entry on a Dampness-Resolving, Joint-Easing Herb
results.original_text: 薏苡仁，味甘，微寒。主筋急拘攣，不可屈伸，風濕痹，下氣。久服輕身益氣。其根下三蟲。生平澤及田野。
results.translation:
Yi Yi Ren [薏苡仁, Coix seed]. Flavor: sweet [甘, gān]. Nature: slightly cold [微寒, wēi hán]. Classical category: superior herb [上品, shàng pǐn].

Primary therapeutic actions [主治, zhǔzhì]: Governs [主] spasm and contracture of the sinews [筋急拘攣], inability to flex or extend the limbs [不可屈伸], wind-damp impediment [風濕痹, fēng-shī bì], and downbearing of qi [下氣]. Prolonged administration [久服] lightens the body [輕身] and augments qi [益氣]. The root expels the three worms [下三蟲, xià sān chóng — classical term for intestinal parasites]. Grows in marshy lowlands [平澤] and open fields [田野].

— Channel tropism [歸經, guī jīng] (post-classical and Kampo elaboration): Lung [肺], Spleen [脾], Stomach [胃], and Kidney [腎] channels.

— Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Sweet [甘].
— Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Slightly cold [微寒].

— Japanese Kampo name [和名]: Yokuinin [ヨクイニン / 薏苡仁]. Classified in Kampo pharmacopoeia (Wakan-yaku) as a dampness-draining, spleen-fortifying medicinal [利水滲濕薬, risui shinsitsu yaku]. Kampo tradition further emphasizes its use in skin disorders — particularly verrucae (warts), eczema, and rough or thickened skin — through its capacity to clear damp-heat from the skin and muscle layers.

— Key classical and Kampo formulas:
  1. Yi Yi Fu Zi San [薏苡附子散] (Jingui Yaolue / Jin Gui Yao Lüè c.220 CE): Coix seed with prepared aconite [附子]; treats chest impediment with cold-damp obstruction.
  2. Yi Yi Fu Zi Bai Jiang San [薏苡附子敗醬散]: Treats intestinal abscess [腸癰] with damp-heat and blood stasis; Coix seed drains pus [排膿] and resolves damp.
  3. Ma Xing Yi Gan Tang [麻杏薏甘湯] (Jingui Yaolue): Wind-damp [風濕] bi-impediment with fever that worsens in the afternoon; uses Coix seed to resolve damp in the muscles and joints.
  4. Sanren Tang [三仁湯] (Wenbing Tiaobian, c.1798): Treats damp-warmth [濕溫] in the upper, middle, and lower burners; Coix seed addresses the lower burner component.
  5. Yokuinin-to [薏苡仁湯] (Kampo): Treats wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome with joint pain, swelling, and restricted movement; frequently used in Japan for rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis.

— Oncological note: In contemporary integrative oncology and Kampo clinical research, Yi Yi Ren / Yokuinin has attracted attention for its constituent coixenolide and related coixol compounds, investigated for anti-tumor and immunomodulatory effects. This usage is not attested in the classical Shennong Bencaojing tradition but represents a modern extension of the 'resolves accumulation and damp-turbidity' [化濁散結] framework.

— Preparation notes: Typically decocted [煎服, jiān fú] at 9–30 g (dried seed, lightly dry-fried [炒, chǎo] for stronger spleen-fortifying action; raw/unprocessed for stronger damp-clearing and anti-inflammatory action). Also prepared as a medicinal congee [薏苡粥] for chronic or convalescent use.

entities_mentioned.name: Yi Yi Ren 薏苡仁
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coix lacryma-jobi L. var. ma-yuen (Roman.) Stapf — Coix seed (Job's tears); family Poaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified classical Chinese medicinals. The seed endosperm is the part used medicinally; the lacquer-hard shell variant is used as beads. Pharmacopoeial standard: Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020) and Japanese Pharmacopoeia both monograph Yokuinin / Yi Yi Ren.

entities_mentioned.name: Wind-damp impediment 風濕痹
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical diagnostic category [bì syndrome, 痹證] encompassing conditions with joint pain, swelling, and restricted movement due to wind, cold, or damp pathogenic factors; broadly corresponds to rheumatic and musculoskeletal disorders in modern terms.

entities_mentioned.name: Three worms 三蟲
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Intestinal parasites (general)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical collective term for internal parasites; the antiparasitic use of Coix root is noted in the Shennong Bencaojing but is rarely employed in modern practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Coixenolide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coixenolide (薏苡仁酯); active lipophilic constituent of Coix lacryma-jobi seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Basis for the injectable pharmaceutical Kanglaite (康萊特注射液), approved in China as an adjunct cancer therapy; reflects the 'damp-resolving, accumulation-scattering' rationale applied to neoplastic disease in modern integrative oncology.

results.translator_notes: The source text supplied is a brief modern summary note ('damp, joint, cancer') rather than a verbatim classical passage; the original_text field therefore provides the canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for 薏苡仁 as reconstructed from the standard received text (Mori Tateyuki recension and Sun Xingyan edition), which is the appropriate classical source for this entry. The translation and elaboration sections synthesize both the Shennong Bencaojing tradition and documented Kampo usage per the entry brief. The oncological dimension ('cancer') in the source label has been addressed under the oncological note, clearly marked as a modern — not classical — application, to avoid anachronistic projection onto the c.200 CE source tradition. Channel tropism (gui jing) is not specified in the original Shennong Bencaojing text and represents later systematization (Ming dynasty onward); this is standard for Plantacopia classical entries.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Yi Yi Ren 薏苡仁

results.title_inferred: Mu Tong (木通): Akebia Stem — Urinary Obstruction, Heart Fire, and Lactation Promotion
results.original_text: 木通 — 味辛，平。主去惡蟲，除脾胃寒熱，通利九竅血脈關節，令人不忘，去惡蟲。久服輕身。一名通草。生山谷。
results.translation:
Mu Tong [木通, 'penetrating wood']. Flavor: acrid [xin, 辛]; bitter [ku, 苦] in later elaborations. Nature: cool [liang, 涼] to cold [han, 寒]. Channel tropism [gui jing, 歸經]: Heart [xin, 心], Small Intestine [xiao chang, 小腸], Bladder [pang guang, 膀胱].

In the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, Mu Tong is described as eliminating malignant parasites [e chong, 惡蟲], dispersing cold and heat in the Spleen and Stomach [pi wei han re, 脾胃寒熱], and promoting free passage through the nine orifices [jiu qiao, 九竅], the blood vessels [xue mai, 血脈], and the joints [guan jie, 關節]. It is said to sharpen memory [ling ren bu wang, 令人不忘]. Prolonged ingestion is recorded as lightening the body [jiu fu qing shen, 久服輕身]. Its alternate classical name is Tong Cao [通草, 'penetrating herb'], a designation later transferred in the Tang and Song periods to a distinct plant (Tetrapanax papyrifer), causing persistent nomenclatural confusion in the textual tradition.

In the mature Kampo [漢方] elaboration (informed by the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目], Tang Ye Bencao [湯液本草], and Japanese Kampo systematization of the Edo period), Mu Tong — identified as Mokutsu [木通] in Japanese — is principally employed for: (1) clearing Heart fire and guiding it downward to the Small Intestine [xin huo xia xing xiao chang, 心火下行小腸], treating oral ulceration, agitation, and restlessness accompanied by dark, burning, or scanty urination [xin fan niao chi, 心煩尿赤]; (2) promoting urination and relieving urinary obstruction [li niao tong lin, 利尿通淋], particularly for hot painful urinary dysfunction [re lin, 熱淋] manifesting as dysuria, hematuria, or urinary gravel; (3) promoting lactation [tong ru, 通乳] by unblocking the channels and collaterals in postpartum women with insufficient milk due to stagnation; (4) unblocking the joints [tong guan jie, 通關節] and relieving painful obstruction [bi zheng, 痹證] associated with damp-heat.

Classical category: Middle herb [zhong pin, 中品] in the tripartite Shennong classification (superior / middle / inferior).

Key classical formulas featuring Mu Tong:
— Ba Zheng San [八正散]: Principal formula for hot painful urinary dysfunction [re lin]; combines Mu Tong with Qu Mai [瞿麥], Bian Xu [萹蓄], Hua Shi [滑石], Zhi Zi [梔子], Da Huang [大黃], Deng Xin Cao [燈心草], and Gan Cao Shao [甘草梢].
— Dao Chi San [導赤散]: Clears Heart fire and promotes urination; combines Mu Tong with Sheng Di Huang [生地黃], Zhu Ye [竹葉], and Gan Cao [甘草]; foundational formula in pediatric Kampo practice.
— Long Dan Xie Gan Tang [龍膽瀉肝湯]: Drains Liver and Gallbladder fire-damp; Mu Tong assists the draining and urinary-promoting action.
— Mu Tong Tang [木通湯] (Kampo variant): Used for postpartum lactation insufficiency with channel stagnation.

Preparation: Typically decocted [jian, 煎] in water, 3–9 g per dose in classical texts; raw form preferred for clearing heat. Contraindicated in pregnancy and in patients with Kidney deficiency without heat signs. Note: Aristolochia manshuriensis (Guan Mu Tong, 關木通), a toxic adulterant containing aristolochic acid, was historically substituted in parts of China and is now banned; authentic Mu Tong refers exclusively to Akebia quinata (Thunb.) Decne. or related Akebia species.

entities_mentioned.name: Mu Tong 木通
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Akebia quinata (Thunb.) Decne. (Lardizabalaceae); also Akebia trifoliata (Thunb.) Koidz. — stem (caulis Akebiae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical name Mu Tong in the Shennong Bencaojing likely referred broadly to woody climbing vines with hollow, channeled stems. By the Song dynasty the identification with Akebia spp. was standardized. Critically, Guan Mu Tong (關木通, Aristolochia manshuriensis) was used as an adulterant in 20th-century Chinese commerce and caused nephrotoxicity due to aristolochic acid; it is pharmacologically and botanically entirely distinct and must not be conflated with authentic Mu Tong.

entities_mentioned.name: Tong Cao 通草
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tetrapanax papyrifer (Hook.) K.Koch — pith (medulla Tetrapanacis); historically confused with Mu Tong
entities_mentioned.notes: In the Shennong Bencaojing, Tong Cao is listed as an alternate name for Mu Tong. From the Tang dynasty onward, Tong Cao was reassigned to Tetrapanax papyrifer, a distinct and milder herb. This nomenclatural shift is a well-documented source of textual confusion in both Chinese and Japanese Kampo literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Ba Zheng San 八正散
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical multi-herb formula for heat-type urinary obstruction (re lin). Sourced from the Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang (太平惠民和劑局方, Song dynasty).

entities_mentioned.name: Dao Chi San 導赤散
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula clearing Heart fire; sourced from Qian Yi's Xiao Er Yao Zheng Zhi Jue (小兒藥證直訣, Song dynasty, c.1119 CE). Foundational in both Chinese pediatrics and Kampo internal medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Mokutsu 木通 (Japanese Kampo name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Akebia quinata or Akebia trifoliata stem, as specified in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: The Japanese Pharmacopoeia (Nihon Yakkyokuho) standardizes Mokutsu as the stem of Akebia quinata or A. trifoliata, explicitly excluding Aristolochia species. Kampo use closely mirrors Chinese classical indications but places particular emphasis on the Dao Chi San application for Heart fire patterns with urinary symptoms.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text as supplied is a modern summary prompt rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage; the original_text field above reconstructs the most likely classical phrasing drawing on the received Shennong Bencaojing (Tao Hongjing recension) and corroborating passages in the Bencao Gangmu (Li Shizhen, 1596). Scholars should verify against critical editions (e.g., Shang Zhijun 尚志鈞 reconstruction).
2. The toxicity issue surrounding Guan Mu Tong / aristolochic acid nephropathy is clinically critical and has been flagged prominently; any archival or clinical use of this entry must account for this distinction.
3. The classical memory-enhancing claim [ling ren bu wang] is noted as a standard Shennong Bencaojing formulaic ascription common to many superior and middle-class herbs and should not be weighted as a primary therapeutic indication in clinical contexts.
4. Channel tropism and Five Flavors as given reflect the post-Song systematization (particularly Zhu Danxi and Li Dongyuan schools) rather than the Shennong Bencaojing itself, which does not use the full gui jing framework; this anachronism is inherent to the Plantacopia schema's requirements and is flagged here for scholarly transparency.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Mu Tong 木通

results.title_inferred: Hua Shi (Talc): Classical Chinese and Kampo Medicinal Entry
results.original_text: 滑石，味甘，寒。主身熱泄澼，女子乳難，癃閉，利小便，蕩胃中積聚寒熱，益精氣。久服輕身，耐飢，長年。生山谷。
results.translation:
Hua Shi [滑石, Talc]. Flavor: sweet [gan 甘]. Nature: cold [han 寒]. Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Bladder [pangguang 膀胱], Stomach [wei 胃], Lung [fei 肺].

Primary actions [zhu zhi 主治]: Governs [zhu 主] bodily heat [shen re 身熱] with diarrhea and dysentery [xie pi 泄澼]; difficulty in lactation [nü zi ru nan 女子乳難]; urinary obstruction [long bi 癃閉]; promotes diuresis [li xiao bian 利小便]; disperses accumulations of cold and heat [ji ju han re 積聚寒熱] lodged within the stomach [wei zhong 胃中]; and replenishes essential qi [yi jing qi 益精氣]. Prolonged administration [jiu fu 久服] lightens the body [qing shen 輕身], confers resistance to hunger [nai ji 耐飢], and prolongs life [chang nian 長年]. Found growing in mountain valleys [sheng shan gu 生山谷].

--- CLASSICAL CATEGORY ---
Classified as a Superior Herb [shang pin 上品] in the Shennong Bencaojing [神農本草經], reflecting its perceived safety for long-term use and tonic properties alongside its more acute therapeutic applications.

--- FIVE FLAVORS (Wu Wei 五味) ---
Sweet [gan 甘]: The sweet flavor enters the Spleen and Stomach, moderates and harmonizes. In Hua Shi, sweetness reflects its gentle, moistening, and downward-directing quality, guiding damp-heat downward and out through the urine.

--- FOUR NATURES (Si Qi 四氣) ---
Cold [han 寒]: The cold nature directly counteracts heat [re 熱] and fire [huo 火] pathologies. Hua Shi is considered markedly cold, making it particularly suited for damp-heat [shi re 濕熱] conditions, summer-heat [shu 暑] patterns, and urinary heat obstruction.

--- CHANNEL TROPISM (Gui Jing 歸經) ---
Primary: Bladder [Pangguang Jing 膀胱經] — principal channel for urinary disorders and the transformation of fluids [qi hua 氣化].
Secondary: Stomach [Wei Jing 胃經] — addresses gastric accumulation, heat in the middle burner [zhong jiao 中焦], and diarrheal conditions.
Tertiary (post-classical elaboration): Lung [Fei Jing 肺經] — reflected in its external topical use for clearing summer-heat through the skin's dispersing function [xuan fa 宣發].

--- THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (Zhi Fa 治法) ---
1. Clears heat and resolves dampness [qing re li shi 清熱利濕]: The foundational action of Hua Shi. It drains damp-heat downward through the bladder, treating hot, painful, or dribbling urination [re lin 熱淋].
2. Promotes urination and opens strangury [li shui tong lin 利水通淋]: Specifically indicated for lin syndrome [lin zheng 淋證] — including heat strangury [re lin], stone strangury [shi lin 石淋], and blood strangury [xue lin 血淋].
3. Expels summer-heat [jie shu 解暑]: A key herb for summer-heat patterns, particularly where damp and heat combine [shu shi 暑濕], causing fever, thirst, scanty dark urine, and diarrhea.
4. Harmonizes the Stomach and arrests diarrhea: Classical usage per the Bencaojing for 'xie pi' [泄澼] — dysenteric or diarrheal disorders arising from damp-heat in the gastrointestinal tract.
5. External application: Absorbs dampness and prevents excoriation [shou shi sheng ji 收濕生肌]; applied topically as a powder to eczema, miliaria [heat rash], and intertrigo.

--- KAMPO (Japanese) ELABORATION ---
Japanese Kampo name: Kasseki [滑石, read as Kasseki in Japanese on'yomi].
In the Kampo tradition, Kasseki retains all classical Chinese indications, with particular emphasis in the following contexts:
- Urinary tract disorders: Featured prominently in formulas addressing painful, hot, or blocked urination, consistent with the Bencaojing's li xiao bian indication.
- Summer-heat syndromes: Used in warm-weather febrile and gastroenteric illnesses.
- Topical use: Powdered Kasseki (often mixed with Gan Cao [甘草, licorice]) applied to skin conditions remains a recognized Kampo application.
Kampo physicians (particularly of the Koho-ha [古方派] classical school) cite Kasseki as an important mineral drug [kinzoku-yaku 金石薬] whose density and sliding [katsu 滑] quality mechanically assists the passage of urinary stones and the downward flow of pathological fluids.

--- KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS ---
1. Liu Yi San [六一散, 'Six-to-One Powder']: Hua Shi (6 parts) + Gan Cao [甘草] (1 part). Quintessential formula for summer-heat with dampness; clears heat, resolves dampness, promotes urination. Described in the Jin-Yuan dynasty Xuanming Lun Fang [宣明論方] of Liu Wansu.
2. Zhu Ling Tang [豬苓湯, 'Polyporus Decoction']: Hua Shi + Zhu Ling [豬苓] + Fu Ling [茯苓] + Ze Xie [澤瀉] + E Jiao [阿膠]. From the Shang Han Lun [傷寒論, c.220 CE]; treats water accumulation with heat, bloody or painful urination, and insomnia.
3. Ba Zheng San [八正散, 'Eight Corrections Powder']: A compound formula for severe heat strangury [re lin] and urinary tract infection with stones; Hua Shi is a principal ingredient alongside Mu Tong [木通], Bian Xu [萹蓄], and others.
4. Hua Shi Bai Yu San [滑石白魚散]: Hua Shi + Bai Yu [白魚, silverfish / fish scales] + Luan Fa [亂髮, charred human hair]. From the Jin Gui Yao Lue [金匱要略]; treats blood strangury [xue lin].
5. Gan Lu Xiao Du Dan [甘露消毒丹]: Classical formula for damp-heat pestilence [shi re wen yi 濕熱瘟疫] affecting the Qi level; Hua Shi is a chief herb.

--- CONTRAINDICATIONS AND CAUTIONS ---
Contraindicated [jin yong 禁用] or used with caution [shen yong 慎用] in: deficiency-cold of the Spleen and Stomach [pi wei xu han 脾胃虛寒]; pregnancy (strong downward-draining action); yin deficiency with internal heat without dampness [yin xu nei re wu shi 陰虛內熱無濕] — the cold, draining nature may injure fluids [shang jin 傷津].

entities_mentioned.name: Hua Shi 滑石
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Talc — hydrated magnesium silicate [Mg₃Si₄O₁₀(OH)₂]; a soft phyllosilicate mineral. Pharmaceutical-grade talc (Talcum) is used both internally in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) preparations and externally as a dusting powder.
entities_mentioned.notes: Modern safety note: industrial talc has been associated with asbestos contamination; pharmaceutical-grade talc used in TCM/Kampo is distinct and subject to quality standards. Internal use should follow appropriate clinical supervision. Hua Shi is official in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (ChP).

entities_mentioned.name: Gan Cao 甘草
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. (Chinese licorice root); also G. glabra or G. inflata accepted per ChP.
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Hua Shi in the canonical Liu Yi San formula; Gan Cao moderates Hua Shi's extreme coldness and protects the Spleen.

entities_mentioned.name: Zhu Ling 豬苓
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyporus umbellatus (Pers.) Fries — a fungus used for its diuretic and immune-modulating properties.
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-herb in Zhu Ling Tang alongside Hua Shi; both have pronounced water-moving [li shui 利水] functions.

entities_mentioned.name: E Jiao 阿膠
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Equus asinus (donkey-hide gelatin); prepared by prolonged boiling of donkey skin.
entities_mentioned.notes: In Zhu Ling Tang, E Jiao nourishes yin and stops bleeding, counterbalancing the draining action of Hua Shi and Zhu Ling in cases of urinary bleeding.

entities_mentioned.name: Liu Yi San 六一散
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical two-ingredient powder formula; not a plant but a compound formula [fufang 複方].
entities_mentioned.notes: Name derives from the 6:1 ratio of Hua Shi to Gan Cao. Foundational summer-heat formula still in common clinical use in TCM and Kampo.

entities_mentioned.name: Zhu Ling Tang 豬苓湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical five-ingredient decoction from the Shang Han Lun [傷寒論] and Jin Gui Yao Lue [金匱要略] of Zhang Zhongjing (c.220 CE).
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most frequently cited Kampo formulas for urinary tract infections with yin deficiency and heat. Hua Shi (Kasseki) is an essential component.

entities_mentioned.name: Ba Zheng San 八正散
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Multi-herb classical formula for urinary heat strangury; documented in the Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang [太平惠民和劑局方] (Song dynasty, 1107 CE).
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely used in modern TCM for acute urinary tract infections and urolithiasis.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: The source segment provided is a modern editorial summary prompt rather than a verbatim Classical Chinese passage from a historical manuscript. The 'original_text' field has been reconstructed from the authentic Shennong Bencaojing [神農本草経] entry for Hua Shi as preserved in the Mao Shijing recension and cross-referenced with the Zhenghe Bencao [證和本草] and Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目] traditions. Scholars should consult: Unschuld, P.U., 'Medicine in China: A History of Ideas' (1985); Bensky, D. et al., 'Chinese Herbal Medicine: Materia Medica' 3rd ed. (2004); and the Xin Jiao Zhu Shennong Bencao Jing [新校注神農本草經] for the primary Chinese critical edition.
2. MINERAL DRUG CATEGORY: Hua Shi is a mineral [kuang wu yao 礦物藥], not a plant. Its inclusion in the Bencaojing alongside botanical and animal drugs reflects the classical Chinese pharmacopoeia's broad material medica scope. The Plantacopia schema's plant-centric fields (e.g., 'modern_identification') have been adapted accordingly.
3. KAMPO READING: The Japanese on'yomi reading of 滑石 is 'Kasseki.' In Kampo clinical texts (e.g., the Koho-ha school's Kohogen [古方源]), the substance is discussed in the context of Zhu Ling Tang (J: Choreito 猪苓湯) most prominently.
4. FIVE FLAVORS DISCREPANCY: Some post-classical commentators (notably Li Shizhen in the Bencao Gangmu, 1596) describe Hua Shi as having both sweet [gan] and bland [dan 淡] flavors. The bland flavor is specifically associated with the leaching/draining of dampness [shen shi 滲濕]. The Bencaojing records only gan; the dan quality represents a Tang-Song period interpretive elaboration.
5. SAFETY: This entry is for scholarly/archival purposes only. Talc for internal medicinal use requires pharmaceutical-grade material and qualified clinical supervision. The translator makes no therapeutic recommendations.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Hua Shi 滑石

results.title_inferred: Yin Chen Hao — Capillary Wormwood: Classical Herb Entry for Jaundice and Liver Conditions
results.original_text: 茵陳蒿，味苦，平。主風濕寒熱，邪氣，熱結黃疸。久服輕身，益氣，耐老。生丘陵阪岸上。（《神農本草經》上品）
results.translation:
Yin Chen Hao [茵陳蒿, Artemisia capillaris]. Flavor: bitter. Nature: neutral [ping, 平].

Primary actions: Governs wind-damp [feng shi, 風濕] cold-heat [han re, 寒熱] disorders, pathogenic qi [xie qi, 邪氣], and heat-binding jaundice [re jie huang dan, 熱結黃疸].

Extended use: Prolonged ingestion lightens the body, augments qi [yi qi, 益氣], and confers resistance to aging [nai lao, 耐老].

Habitat note: Grows on hills, slopes, and embankments.

Classical category: Superior herb [shang pin, 上品] — deemed non-toxic and suitable for long-term use.

---
CHINESE NAME: Yin Chen Hao (茵陳蒿)
JAPANESE KAMPO NAME: Inchin (インチン / 茵陳); also rendered Inchin-kō (茵陳蒿)
FIVE FLAVORS [Wu Wei, 五味]: Bitter [ku, 苦]; secondary sweet [gan, 甘] in later elaborations
FOUR NATURES [Si Qi, 四氣]: Slightly cold [wei han, 微寒] — the Shennong Bencaojing gives 'neutral/balanced [ping]', but Tang and later Song dynasty commentators (e.g., Tao Hongjing, Su Song) standardized it as slightly cold, reflecting its clinical use in clearing damp-heat [shi re, 濕熱]
CHANNEL TROPISM [Gui Jing, 歸經]: Spleen [pi, 脾], Stomach [wei, 胃], Liver [gan, 肝], Gallbladder [dan, 膽] — channel assignments codified in the Jin-Yuan and Ming periods

THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (classical and Kampo elaboration):
1. Clears heat and eliminates dampness [qing re li shi, 清熱利濕]: The principal action; drains damp-heat accumulation from the liver and gallbladder.
2. Resolves jaundice [tui huang, 退黃]: The foremost classical herb for icteric conditions of both the yang [yang huang, 陽黃, bright-yellow, damp-heat type] and, in combination, the yin [yin huang, 陰黃, dark-yellow, cold-damp type] variety.
3. Clears liver fire and benefits the gallbladder [qing gan li dan, 清肝利膽]: Promotes bile secretion and flow; used for biliary disorders.
4. Dispels wind-damp [qu feng shi, 祛風濕]: Early classical indication per the Shennong Bencaojing tradition.
5. Diuretic action [li niao, 利尿]: Facilitates resolution of damp accumulation via urination.

KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS:
- Yin Chen Hao Tang (茵陳蒿湯) [Zhang Zhongjing, Shang Han Lun / Jin Gui Yao Lue, c.220 CE]: Yin Chen Hao + Zhi Zi (Gardenia) + Da Huang (Rhubarb). The foundational formula for yang-type (damp-heat) jaundice with fever, concentrated dark urine, and constipation.
- Yin Chen Wu Ling San (茵陳五苓散) [Jin Gui Yao Lue]: Yin Chen Hao combined with the Wu Ling San formula; addresses jaundice with concurrent water accumulation and difficult urination.
- Yin Chen Si Ni Tang (茵陳四逆湯) [later elaboration]: Yin Chen Hao + Fu Zi + Gan Jiang + Gan Cao; for yin-type cold-damp jaundice.

KAMPO NOTES (Japanese elaboration):
In Kampo medicine, Inchin (茵陳) remains central to the treatment of hepatobiliary disease. The Inchin-kō-tō (茵陳蒿湯, direct transliteration of Yin Chen Hao Tang) is a standard Kampo prescription approved in Japan for hepatitis, cholecystitis, liver cirrhosis with jaundice, and nephrotic syndrome with edema. Kampo texts such as the Honchō Shokkan (本朝食鑑, 1697) and commentaries by Yoshimasu Tōdō (吉益東洞, 1702–1773) emphasize the herb's action on 'heat lurking in the blood level' [chi netsu, 血熱] as well as its diuretic clearing function. The young spring shoots [yin chen, 茵陳 — literally 'old-stem new-growth'] are considered medicinally superior, giving rise to the classical saying: '三月茵陳四月蒿，五月六月當柴燒' ('In the third month it is Yin Chen [medicine]; in the fourth month merely Hao [wormwood]; by the fifth and sixth months it is only firewood').

entities_mentioned.name: 茵陳蒿 (Yin Chen Hao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia capillaris Thunb. (Capillary wormwood / Capillary artemisia); also sometimes A. scoparia Waldst. & Kit. used interchangeably in trade
entities_mentioned.notes: Asteraceae family. The young overwintered shoots harvested in early spring are the pharmacopeially preferred material. The active constituents include capillarisin, chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, and scoparone (6,7-dimethoxycoumarin), the last of which has demonstrated choleretic, hepatoprotective, and anti-inflammatory activity in modern pharmacological studies, supporting the classical hepatobiliary indications.

entities_mentioned.name: 茵陳蒿湯 (Yin Chen Hao Tang / Inchin-kō-tō)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula: Artemisia capillaris + Gardenia jasminoides + Rheum palmatum
entities_mentioned.notes: Sourced from both Shang Han Lun (傷寒論) and Jin Gui Yao Lue (金匱要略) by Zhang Zhongjing. Remains one of the most clinically studied classical formulas in hepatology in East Asia.

entities_mentioned.name: 茵陳五苓散 (Yin Chen Wu Ling San)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound formula combining Yin Chen Hao with Wu Ling San (Polyporus, Alisma, Atractylodes, Poria, Cinnamon twig)
entities_mentioned.notes: Indicated for jaundice accompanied by fluid retention and oliguria; reflects the dual damp-clearing and diuretic strategy.

results.translator_notes: The source segment was a brief botanical label rather than a full classical passage; accordingly, this entry reconstructs a comprehensive Plantacopia record from the Shennong Bencaojing tradition (c.200 CE), Tang and Song dynasty commentary, Jin-Yuan channel-tropism theory, and Kampo (Edo-period Japanese) elaboration. The original Shennong Bencaojing flavor/nature given is bitter and neutral [ping]; the later clinical tradition's refinement to 'slightly cold' is noted and distinguished from the ur-text. The colloquial saying about seasonal harvesting is attested in multiple Ming and Qing materia medica sources (e.g., Bencao Gangmu supplementary notes) and is preserved here as culturally and pharmacognostically significant. No OCR errors were present in the source as it was a clean label; no illegible passages exist.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Yin Chen Hao 茵陳蒿

results.title_inferred: Jin Qian Cao — Moneywort / Creeping Jenny: Classical and Kampo Entry
results.original_text: 金錢草 — Lysimachia — stones, jaundice, urinary
results.translation: Jin Qian Cao (金錢草, 'Golden Coin Herb'): Chinese Name: Jīn Qián Cǎo (金錢草). Japanese Kampo Name: Kin-sen-sō (金銭草). Modern Botanical Identification: Lysimachia christinae Hance (Family Primulaceae; historically also conflated with Desmodium styracifolium and regional variants). Classical Category: Not formally listed among the 365 entries of the received Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE) by name; enters the mainstream materia medica tradition primarily through later Tang–Song compilations (notably the Bencao Gangmu Shiyi and regional southern Chinese herbals), and was subsequently integrated into Qing-dynasty and modern Chinese pharmacopoeia. In Kampo tradition it is classified as a supplementary (shi yi 拾遺) herb rather than a canonical superior/middle/inferior grade herb. Five Flavors (Wǔ Wèi 五味): Sweet (gān 甘) and slightly salty (xián 鹹), with some classical sources also noting a mild bitter (kǔ 苦) quality. Four Natures (Sì Qì 四氣): Cool (liáng 涼). Channel Tropism (Guī Jīng 歸經): Liver (Gān 肝), Gallbladder (Dǎn 膽), Kidney (Shèn 腎), Bladder (Páng Guāng 膀胱). Classical and Kampo Therapeutic Actions: (1) Clears Heat and Resolves Dampness (qīng rè lì shī 清熱利濕): The herb is considered foremost among agents that discharge damp-heat lodged in the lower burner (xia jiao 下焦), the urinary tract, and the biliary system. It promotes the free flow of urine and bile, thereby relieving the accumulation responsible for stone formation. (2) Expels Urinary and Biliary Stones (pái shí 排石): Classically described as softening and expelling 'sand and stones' (shā shí 砂石) obstructing the urinary passages (lín zhèng 淋證) and bile ducts. Indicated for all five categories of urinary strangury (wǔ lín 五淋), especially stone strangury (shí lín 石淋) and turbid strangury (gāo lín 膏淋). (3) Clears Liver and Gallbladder Damp-Heat; Treats Jaundice (lì dǎn tuì huáng 利膽退黃): Used in the pattern of yang jaundice (yáng huáng 陽黃) where damp-heat congests the liver and gallbladder, producing icteric yellowing of the skin and eyes, dark urine, and flank distension. Kampo elaborations specifically pair it with hepatobiliary stasis presentations. (4) Reduces Swelling and Resolves Toxin (xiāo zhǒng jiě dú 消腫解毒): Topical application (as fresh-pounded poultice) and internal decoction both noted for snakebite, abscesses, and externally contracted swellings in southern Chinese and Japanese regional traditions. (5) Invigorates Blood and Unblocks the Channels (huó xuè tōng luò 活血通絡): Secondary action recognized in Kampo and Qing-era sources, relevant to trauma-related swelling and early-stage nodular accumulations. Preparation Methods: Primary preparation is aqueous decoction (jiān jì 煎劑); fresh herb juice pressed and administered directly is classical for acute stone presentations and topical toxin conditions. Dried herb may also be taken as powder (sǎn 散) infused in warm water. Dosage in classical references: 15–60 g dried herb per decoction; fresh herb up to 120–250 g. Key Classical and Kampo Formulas: (1) Jīn Qián Cǎo Tāng (金錢草湯): Decoction centered on this herb for urinary stone strangury, often combined with Hǎi Jīn Shā (海金沙, Lygodium spores), Jī Nèi Jīn (雞內金, Gallus gallus domesticus endothelium corneum), and Shí Wéi (石葦, Pyrrosia leaf). (2) Paired with Yīn Chén Hāo (茵陳蒿) and Zhī Zǐ (梔子) in jaundice presentations analogous to the Yīn Chén Hāo Tāng framework. (3) In Kampo (Japanese) practice, Kin-sen-sō is frequently incorporated into Gosha-jinki-gan (牛車腎氣丸) adjunct protocols for urolithiasis and combined with Takusha (澤瀉, Alisma) and Bukuryo (茯苓, Poria) for lower burner damp-heat patterns. Conditions Treated (Classical Terminology): Shí lín (石淋, stone strangury / urolithiasis); Rè lín (熱淋, heat strangury / acute urinary tract infection); Huáng dǎn (黃疸, jaundice, particularly damp-heat type); Dǎn dào jié shí (膽道結石, biliary calculi); Shé chóng dǎo shāng (蛇蟲咬傷, snakebite and insect sting, topical).

entities_mentioned.name: Jin Qian Cao (金錢草)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lysimachia christinae Hance (Primulaceae); regional variants include Desmodium styracifolium (Fabaceae) in Lingnan tradition and Dichondra repens in some Taiwanese sources
entities_mentioned.notes: The canonical pharmacopoeia identification in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020 edition) is Lysimachia christinae Hance. Significant regional and historical variation in the plants grouped under this name warrants caution in cross-referencing pre-Qing sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Hai Jin Sha (海金沙)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lygodium japonicum spores (Lygodiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequent formula partner for stone strangury; enhances urinary discharge and stone-expelling action.

entities_mentioned.name: Ji Nei Jin (雞內金)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Endothelium corneum gigeriae galli — dried inner lining of chicken gizzard
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical softening and dissolving agent for urinary and gallbladder stones.

entities_mentioned.name: Shi Wei (石葦)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pyrrosia lingua / Pyrrosia sheareri (Polypodiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears heat, promotes urination, and aids stone expulsion; standard companion herb in lín syndrome formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Yin Chen Hao (茵陳蒿)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia capillaris Thunb. (Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal herb for damp-heat jaundice; classical pairing with Jin Qian Cao for biliary stone and jaundice presentations.

entities_mentioned.name: Kin-sen-sō (金銭草) — Kampo name
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Same as Jin Qian Cao: Lysimachia christinae
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in Kampo adjunct protocols for urolithiasis and hepatobiliary damp-heat; not a standalone classical Kampo formula herb in the Edo-period canon, but integrated into modern Kampo clinical practice.

results.translator_notes: Jin Qian Cao does not appear by this specific name in the received 365-herb Shennong Bencaojing text; it enters the canonical literature through later compilations (Tang Bencao, Bencao Gangmu supplementary volumes, and Qing-era southern regional herbals). The entry has been constructed from the Shennong Bencaojing tradition's theoretical framework (Five Flavors, Four Natures, channel tropism) applied to the herb as it appears in subsequent classical elaborations, which is standard practice for herbs formally recognized post-Han. The source segment provided is a minimal stub ('Lysimachia — stones, jaundice, urinary'); all classical properties, formula citations, preparation details, and Kampo elaborations have been generated from established sinological and pharmacognostic scholarship rather than direct OCR of a primary text. No illegible passages to flag. Researchers should consult the Bencao Gangmu (1596, Li Shizhen) chapter on 草部 (Herb Section) and the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020) monograph for authoritative sourcing.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Jin Qian Cao 金錢草

results.title_inferred: Hai Jin Sha (Sea Gold Sand) — Lygodium Spores for Urinary Disorders
results.original_text: 海金沙 — 性寒，味甘淡，入小腸、膀胱經。主治小便不利，熱淋，石淋，血淋，膏淋，尿道澀痛。清利濕熱，通淋止痛。
results.translation:
Hai Jin Sha [Sea Gold Sand] (海金沙)

**Chinese Name:** Hǎi Jīn Shā (海金沙)
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Kakinsha (海金沙)
**Classical Category:** Middle-grade herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — principally medicinal in action, used to treat illness without pronounced toxicity at standard doses.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Sweet [甘, gān] and bland [淡, dàn].

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Cold [寒, hán].

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Enters the Small Intestine [小腸] and Urinary Bladder [膀胱] channels.

**Primary Therapeutic Actions:**
Clears Heat and drains Dampness [清熱利濕]; promotes urination and relieves strangury [通淋止痛].

Classically indicated for:
— Inhibited urination [小便不利, xiǎo biàn bù lì]: difficult or scanty urination due to Damp-Heat accumulation in the lower burner.
— Hot strangury [熱淋, rè lín]: burning, urgent, painful urination with dark yellow urine.
— Stone strangury [石淋, shí lín]: urinary calculi (stones) causing severe colicky pain, hematuria, and obstruction — the condition for which Hai Jin Sha is most prominently featured.
— Blood strangury [血淋, xuè lín]: blood in the urine with pain and heat signs.
— Milky strangury [膏淋, gāo lín]: turbid or chylous urine.
— Urethral astringent pain [尿道澀痛]: a burning, rough, obstructed sensation during urination.

**Preparation Methods:**
The dried spores of Lygodium japonicum are typically administered as a decoction [煎服, jiān fú], wrapped in cheesecloth or a herb bag [包煎, bāo jiān] during boiling to prevent the fine powdery spores from making the liquid turbid and difficult to ingest. Dosage in classical formulation: 6–15 g per decoction. May also be ground into powder and swallowed directly with water.

**Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:**
— Hai Jin Sha San [海金沙散]: Hai Jin Sha combined with Talcum [滑石, huá shí] and Licorice [甘草, gān cǎo]; classical formula for hot strangury and stone strangury.
— Er Jin San [二金散]: Hai Jin Sha paired with Chicken Gold [雞內金, jī nèi jīn]; used specifically for urinary and biliary stones, highly regarded in both Chinese and Kampo traditions.
— In Kampo practice, Hai Jin Sha is incorporated into modified formulations for urolithiasis (尿路結石) alongside Akebia [木通], Plantago seed [車前子], and Achyranthes [牛膝], following the principle of guiding medicinals downward to expel stones through the urinary tract.

**Shennong Bencaojing Tradition Note:**
Hai Jin Sha does not appear by name in the original Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE), as its formal materia medica description emerges in later texts, notably the Jiayou Bencao [嘉祐本草, c. 1060 CE] and is elaborated in the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目, 1596 CE] by Li Shizhen, who systematized its use for the five stranguries [五淋]. The Kampo tradition received and refined this knowledge through the Edo period, where it became a standard ingredient in urinary stone formulations.

entities_mentioned.name: 海金沙 (Hǎi Jīn Shā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lygodium japonicum (Thunb.) Sw. — spores of the Japanese climbing fern, family Lygodiaceae (formerly Schizaeaceae). The medicinal part is the dried, ripe spore powder, which appears as a fine gold-colored dust — hence the name 'Sea Gold Sand.'
entities_mentioned.notes: Modern pharmacological research confirms diuretic, stone-dissolving, and urinary antispasmodic properties, supporting classical indications. Active constituents include flavonoids and fatty acids. Used in contemporary Traditional Chinese Medicine and Kampo for urolithiasis (kidney and ureteral stones) and urinary tract infections.

entities_mentioned.name: 滑石 (Huá Shí) — Talcum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Talc (hydrated magnesium silicate, Mg3Si4O10(OH)2)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequent co-herb with Hai Jin Sha in strangury formulas; also cold in nature, entering the Bladder channel, promoting urination and clearing Damp-Heat.

entities_mentioned.name: 雞內金 (Jī Nèi Jīn) — Chicken's Gizzard Lining
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried inner membrane of the chicken gizzard (Gallus gallus domesticus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Hai Jin Sha in Er Jin San for dissolving urinary and biliary stones; believed to strengthen digestive function and promote stone dissolution through enzymatic action.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a compressed modern summary label rather than a direct classical Chinese passage. This entry has been reconstructed and expanded from the established Shennong Bencaojing / Bencao Gangmu / Kampo scholarly tradition for Hai Jin Sha, as no verbatim classical text was supplied. The herb's absence from the original Shennong Bencaojing corpus is noted explicitly in the translation for scholarly accuracy — its classical pedigree runs through Song and Ming dynasty materia medica texts. The wrapping instruction (包煎, bāo jiān) is a critically important preparation note preserved from classical pharmacy manuals and should be retained in any clinical or archival context. All technical terms are rendered in both English and Chinese with pinyin for cross-referencing.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Hai Jin Sha 海金沙

results.title_inferred: Shi Wei (Stone Reed Leaf): Classical Chinese and Kampo Monograph on Pyrrosia Leaf
results.original_text: 石葦 — 味苦，平。主勞熱邪氣，五癃閉不通，利小便水道。生山谷石上。
results.translation:
Shi Wei [Stone Reed; 石葦]. Flavor: bitter [ku]. Nature: neutral/level [ping]. Primary actions: governs [zhu] taxation-heat [lao re] and pathogenic qi [xie qi]; treats the five types of urinary blockage and obstruction [wu long bi bu tong]; promotes urination [li xiao bian] and opens the water pathways [shui dao]. Grows on rocks in mountain valleys [sheng shan gu shi shang].

— Classical Category (Shennong Bencaojing): Middle Herb [zhong pin; 中品]. Herbs of this class may have mild toxicity and are used therapeutically for specific conditions over defined treatment periods.

— Five Flavors [wu wei]: Bitter [ku 苦]. Bitter flavor enters the Heart channel; drains, dries, and descends. In this context it clears heat from the lower burner and promotes urination.

— Four Natures [si qi]: Neutral [ping 平]. Neither warming nor cooling in strong degree; suitable for a range of constitutional types. Later elaborations (Tang Bencao, Bencao Gangmu) add a mild cooling quality [wei han], consistent with its clinical use in heat patterns.

— Channel Tropism [gui jing]: Lung [Fei 肺], Bladder [Pangguang 膀胱], Kidney [Shen 腎]. The Lung governs the upper source of water metabolism (tong tiao shui dao); the Bladder is the organ of urinary storage and excretion; the Kidney governs the lower water pathways. Shi Wei acts across all three to free obstruction at every level of the water metabolism axis.

— Therapeutic Actions (classical synthesis):
  1. Clears Lung heat and descends Lung qi [qing Fei re, su Fei qi]: Used in cough with heat, blood-streaked sputum [ke xue], and dyspnea associated with Lung heat constraint.
  2. Promotes urination and unblocks lin syndrome [li shui tong lin]: Principal herb for all varieties of lin [urinary dribbling/obstruction], particularly stone-lin [shi lin], blood-lin [xue lin], and heat-lin [re lin].
  3. Cools the Blood and stops bleeding [liang xue zhi xue]: Addresses hematuria [niao xue], metrorrhagia, and hemoptysis arising from Blood-heat patterns.
  4. Expels urinary stones [pai shi]: A secondary classical indication reinforced in Song and Ming dynasty commentaries; paired with herbs such as Jin Qian Cao [Lysimachia] in later practice.

— Japanese Kampo Name: Sekii [石葦, せきい]. Used in the Kampo tradition within formulas for painful urinary dysfunction [rin shō, 淋症], hematuria, and cough with Blood. Kampo sources (Honzō Kōmoku Keimō and Wakan Sansai Zue) affirm the Bencaojing indications and emphasize topical use of the leaf as a styptic.

— Key Classical Formulas:
  • Shi Wei San [石葦散, Stone Reed Powder] — principal formula; treats stone-lin and heat-lin; combines Shi Wei with Qu Mai [Dianthus], Dong Kui Zi [Malva seed], Hua Shi [Talc], and Che Qian Zi [Plantago seed].
  • Ba Zheng San [八正散] — broader urinary heat formula in which Shi Wei is sometimes substituted or added for pronounced heat-lin with hematuria.
  • Zhu Ling Tang [猪苓湯] variant elaborations in Kampo incorporate Shi Wei for concurrent Lung-dryness cough with urinary disruption.

— Preparation Methods (classical):
  • Remove the hairy underside of the leaf [qu mao] before decoction; the hairs were considered irritating to the throat and Lung.
  • Standard decoction [jian]: 6–12 g dried leaf.
  • Powdered form [san]: ground and taken with warm water for stone-lin.
  • Fresh leaf juice [xian zhi]: applied topically as a styptic for surface bleeding in some regional traditions.

— Modern Identification: Pyrrosia lingua (Thunb.) Farw. (family Polypodiaceae), and related species P. sheareri and P. petiolosa; all used interchangeably under the pharmacopoeial rubric Shi Wei [Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020]. High confidence identification.

entities_mentioned.name: 石葦 Shi Wei
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pyrrosia lingua (Thunb.) Farw. and related Pyrrosia spp. (Polypodiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020 officially recognizes P. lingua, P. sheareri, and P. petiolosa as legitimate sources. The plant grows epiphytically on rocks and tree bark in moist mountain habitats throughout East Asia, consistent with the classical habitat description 'grows on rocks in mountain valleys.'

entities_mentioned.name: Shi Wei San 石葦散
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula featuring Shi Wei as chief herb [jun yao]. Recorded in the Song dynasty Tai Ping Hui Min He Ji Ju Fang [太平惠民和劑局方] and referenced in numerous subsequent materia medica. Standard treatment for lin syndrome with urinary stones or hematuria.

entities_mentioned.name: Qu Mai 瞿麥
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dianthus superbus L. or D. chinensis L. (Caryophyllaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequent formula partner with Shi Wei in urinary heat and lin patterns; promotes urination and clears heat.

entities_mentioned.name: Hua Shi 滑石
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Talcum (hydrated magnesium silicate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mineral component of Shi Wei San; slippery nature assists passage of urinary stones.

entities_mentioned.name: Che Qian Zi 車前子
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago asiatica L. seed (Plantaginaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Diuretic seed; standard component of urinary-clearing formulas alongside Shi Wei.

entities_mentioned.name: Sekii 石葦 (Kampo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pyrrosia lingua and related spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Japanese Kampo name is a direct phonetic rendering of the Chinese; indications in Kampo texts closely mirror the Bencaojing source, with additional commentary in Honzō Kōmoku Keimō on the necessity of removing leaf hairs prior to preparation.

results.translator_notes: The source text supplied is a modern descriptive label rather than a verbatim classical passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the core Shennong Bencaojing entry in classical Chinese (attested in standard critical editions of the Bencaojing), and the translation field synthesizes the full monograph drawing on canonical sources: Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE), Tang Bencao (659 CE), Bencao Yanyi (1116 CE), Bencao Gangmu (1596 CE), and Kampo sources including Honzō Kōmoku Keimō and Wakan Sansai Zue. The instruction to 'remove the hairy underside' (去毛, qu mao) is a consistent processing note across all major materia medica and is clinically important. The Five Natures characterization as 'neutral' (平, ping) in the Bencaojing is refined to 'slightly cold' (微寒, wei han) in later sources; both readings are flagged. No genuinely illegible passages in source; the source label was minimal and has been expanded from attested classical literature for scholarly completeness.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Shi Wei 石葦

results.title_inferred: Bi Xie (萆薢) — Dioscorea Rhizome: Classical Entry on Damp Turbid Urine and Joint Disorders
results.original_text: 萆薢，味苦，平。主腰背痛，強骨節，風寒濕周痹，惡瘡不瘳，熱氣。久服輕身，耐老。生山谷。
results.translation:
Bi Xie [萆薢; Dioscorea rhizome]. Flavor: bitter [ku 苦]. Nature: neutral/balanced [ping 平]. Primary [classical] indications: pain of the lumbar back [yaobei tong 腰背痛]; strengthening of the bone joints [qiang gujie 強骨節]; wind-cold-damp [fenghan shi 風寒濕] generalized painful obstruction [zhou bi 周痹]; malign sores [e chuang 惡瘡] that fail to heal; and pathogenic heat qi [re qi 熱氣]. Prolonged administration lightens the body [qing shen 輕身] and confers resistance to aging [nai lao 耐老]. It grows in mountain valleys [sheng shanju 生山谷].

[Japanese Kampo elaboration:] Known in Kampo medicine as Hikkaige (ヒカカイ / 萆薢). Classified among the middle-grade herbs [zhongpin 中品] of the Shennong Bencaojing tradition. Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Kidney [Shen 腎] and Stomach [Wei 胃] channels, with some classical sources additionally citing the Bladder [Pangguang 膀胱] channel. In Kampo practice, the herb's primary action is to resolve dampness and clarify turbidity in the lower burner [xia jiao 下焦], making it the cardinal herb for 'turbid painful urinary dysfunction' [gao lin 膏淋 / nigo nyō 濁った小便]. It dispels wind-damp [qu fengshi 祛風濕] and unblocks the sinews and joints [tong jingmai 通經脈], addressing bi-syndrome [bi zheng 痹證] manifesting as joint heaviness, soreness, and limited mobility. It also clears damp-heat from the skin, assisting in the resolution of chronic sores and skin lesions that fail to resolve. Key classical formula: Bi Xie Fen Qing Yin [萆薢分清飲], which separates turbid from clear in urinary disorders and remains a foundational Kampo prescription for chronic cloudy urine, white turbid discharge, and chyluria-like conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 萆薢 (Bi Xie)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea hypoglauca Palib. or Dioscorea tokoro Makino (rhizome); family Dioscoreaceae. Some classical sources may conflate with Smilax species (土茯苓 / Smilax glabra), but the mainstream Shennong and Kampo tradition assigns 萆薢 firmly to Dioscorea spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Two primary species are recognized in Chinese Pharmacopoeia: Dioscorea hypoglauca (fen bi xie 粉萆薢) and Dioscorea futschauensis (mian bi xie 綿萆薢). Japanese Kampo sources generally align with D. tokoro or D. hypoglauca. Care should be taken not to confuse 萆薢 with 土茯苓 (Smilax glabra), which treats similar conditions but is a distinct materia medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Bi Xie Fen Qing Yin (萆薢分清飲)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula; not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Formula recorded in Yang Shi's Zhengzhi Zhunsheng (證治準繩, Ming dynasty) and later adopted into Kampo. Principal formula for damp-turbid urinary disorders featuring Bi Xie as the sovereign herb, combined with Yi Zhi Ren (益智仁), Wu Yao (烏藥), and Shi Chang Pu (石菖蒲). A variant formula appears in the Danxi Xinfa (丹溪心法) tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Zhou Bi (周痹 — generalized painful obstruction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corresponds broadly to diffuse arthralgia/arthritis, possibly including rheumatoid or osteoarthritic presentations; also encompasses peripheral neuropathic pain in some classical readings.
entities_mentioned.notes: Zhou Bi is distinguished in the Lingshu (靈樞) from ju bi (局痹, localized obstruction); its inclusion here reflects Bi Xie's classical scope for systemic damp-type joint disorders.

results.translator_notes: The source text supplied was a brief editorial note ('Plant: Bi Xie 萆薢 — Dioscorea — damp turbid urine, joint') rather than a direct Classical Chinese passage. The original_text field above supplies the reconstructed canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for 萆薢 based on received text traditions (consistent across Mori Tateyuki's 1844 reconstruction and the Zhonghua Shuju critical edition), marked as such for scholarly transparency. The translation and Kampo elaboration draw on the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596), the Kampo standard materia medica Honzo Komoku Keimo (本草綱目啓蒙, Ono Ranzan, 1803), and the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020 ed.). The pinyin romanization follows Mandarin standard; the Japanese on-reading Hikkaige reflects Sino-Japanese pronunciation conventions. Classical category 'middle herb' (zhongpin) attribution is consistent across major received editions of the Shennong Bencaojing.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Bi Xie 萆薢

results.title_inferred: Xiang Fu (Cyperus Rhizome): Liver Qi Regulation, Menstrual Disorders, and Pain
results.original_text: 香附 — 莎草科植物莎草 Cyperus rotundus L. 之根莖。味辛、微苦、微甘，性平。歸肝、脾、三焦經。主疏肝解鬱，調經止痛，理氣寬中。
results.translation:
Xiang Fu [香附, 'fragrant appendage'] is the rhizome of Cyperus rotundus L. (family Cyperaceae), known in Japanese Kampo medicine as Kōbu (香附) or Kobushi. 

Chinese Name: Xiāng Fù (香附). The drug is also historically referenced under the names Shā Cǎo (莎草, 'sedge herb') and Què Tóu Xiāng (雀頭香, 'sparrow-head fragrance') in early materia medica texts, including the Mingyi Bielu (名醫別錄, c. 220–450 CE); the rhizome as a distinct medicinal entity was more fully elaborated in the Tang and Song periods, though roots of its therapeutic use appear in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition. 

Classical Category: Middle-grade herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing tripartite classification — herbs of this grade tonify without strong toxicity but are used therapeutically rather than for longevity cultivation alone. 

Japanese Kampo Name: Kōbu (香附, こうぶ); the processed sliced rhizome is termed Kōbushi (香附子, こうぶし). 

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Acrid [辛, xīn] (primary), slightly bitter [微苦, wēi kǔ], slightly sweet [微甘, wēi gān]. The acrid flavor governs dispersing and moving actions; the slight bitterness descends and drains; the slight sweetness harmonizes the center. 

Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Neutral / Balanced [平, píng] — neither markedly warming nor cooling, making it broadly applicable across constitutional types. 

Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Liver [肝], Spleen [脾], Triple Burner [三焦, sān jiāo]. Primary tropism is to the Liver channel, governing the smooth flow of qi [疏肝, shū gān]. 

Therapeutic Actions [主治, zhǔ zhì]: 
1. Courses the Liver and resolves constraint [疏肝解鬱, shū gān jiě yù]: Indicated for Liver qi stagnation [肝氣鬱結] manifesting as hypochondriac and chest oppression, distending pain in the flanks and breast, emotional depression, and sighing. 
2. Regulates menstruation and alleviates pain [調經止痛, tiáo jīng zhǐ tòng]: A foremost herb for gynecological disorders attributed to Liver qi stagnation — dysmenorrhea [痛經], irregular menstruation [月經不調], premenstrual breast tenderness, and amenorrhea. Li Shizhen (Bencao Gangmu, 1578) called it 'the chief of qi medicines among women' [氣病之總司，女科之主帥]. 
3. Moves qi and broadens the Middle Burner [理氣寬中, lǐ qì kuān zhōng]: Addresses epigastric and abdominal distension, focal fullness [痞滿], and poor appetite arising from qi stagnation in the Spleen and Stomach. 
4. Disperses stagnation and alleviates pain [散結止痛]: Used for plum-pit qi [梅核氣], scrofula, and nodular masses associated with phlegm-qi binding. 

Preparation Methods: Raw rhizome [生香附] is used for moving qi and opening constraint. Vinegar-processed [醋製香附, cù zhì] enhances the herb's entry into the Liver channel and strengthens pain-alleviating effects — the classical standard for gynecological applications. Wine-processed [酒製] raises the herb's action and assists circulation. Salt-processed [鹽製] directs the herb downward to the lower burner. Charred [炒炭] may be used to astringe in bleeding with stagnation. 

Key Classical Formulas: 
- Yue Ju Wan [越鞠丸] (Danxi Xinfa, Zhu Danxi, 1347): Xiang Fu paired with Cang Zhu, Chuan Xiong, Zhi Zi, and Shen Qu — the canonical formula for the six stagnations [六鬱]. 
- Xiang Fu Wan [香附丸] (various Song–Ming sources): Base formula for Liver qi gynecological conditions. 
- Si Hai Shu Yu Wan [四海舒郁丸]: Includes Xiang Fu for goiter and nodular swellings. 
- Ju He Wan [橘核丸]: Flanking herb for cold-type hernia pain with qi stagnation. 
- In Kampo, Xiang Fu appears prominently in Kōso-san (香蘇散, Xiāng Sū Sǎn) — Xiang Fu with Zi Su Ye, Chen Pi, Zhi Gan Cao — used for exterior cold with interior qi stagnation, common cold with emotional depression, and gastrointestinal upset; this formula remains in official Japanese Kampo pharmacopoeia use. 

Contraindications and Cautions: Contraindicated or used cautiously in cases of qi deficiency without stagnation, and in yin deficiency with heat signs, as the acrid-moving nature may further consume fluids. Use with caution during pregnancy in large doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Xiang Fu (香附)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyperus rotundus L. — rhizome (Cyperaceae); commonly known as nutsedge or purple nutsedge
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most thoroughly botanically confirmed herbs in the Chinese materia medica tradition. The rhizome (not the aerial parts) is the medicinal portion. Cyperus rotundus is a cosmopolitan weed with a long history of medicinal use across Asian, Middle Eastern, and Ayurvedic traditions (where it is known as Musta or Nagarmotha).

entities_mentioned.name: Sha Cao (莎草)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyperus rotundus L. — the whole sedge plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Archaic name for the whole plant; the rhizome specifically as 'Xiang Fu' or 'Que Tou Xiang' became standardized later.

entities_mentioned.name: Kōbushi / Kōbu (香附子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyperus rotundus L. rhizome — Japanese Kampo designation
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia and standard Kampo formularies. Particularly prominent in Kōso-san (香蘇散).

entities_mentioned.name: Yue Ju Wan (越鞠丸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Chinese formula — no single botanical equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: Attributed to Zhu Danxi (朱丹溪), c. 1347. Formula for resolving the six stagnations (qi, blood, phlegm, fire, dampness, food). Xiang Fu addresses the qi stagnation component.

entities_mentioned.name: Kōso-san (香蘇散 / Xiāng Sū Sǎn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kampo/Chinese classical formula
entities_mentioned.notes: Included in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia as an official Kampo preparation (extract granules). Indicated for cold, headache, and gastrointestinal symptoms with emotional tension.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a brief modern summary label rather than a classical Chinese primary text passage. This entry has therefore been reconstructed as a comprehensive Plantacopia record synthesizing: (1) the Shennong Bencaojing tradition and subsequent Tang/Song elaborations (Xinxiu Bencao, Bencao Yanyi); (2) the authoritative Li Shizhen synthesis in Bencao Gangmu (1578), which contains the famous characterization of Xiang Fu as the pre-eminent women's qi medicine; and (3) Japanese Kampo elaboration, particularly the use of Kōbushi in Kōso-san and related formulas. Xiang Fu does not appear prominently as a named drug in the original Shennong Bencaojing (it is referenced indirectly under Sha Cao / 莎草), and its full classical elaboration is properly a product of the Tang through Ming dynasties. This temporal layering has been noted in the translation. No OCR errors were detected in the source label. The Five Flavors, Four Natures, and channel tropism recorded here reflect the consensus of the Zhongyao Da Cidian (中藥大辭典) and Japanese Kampo pharmacopoeia standards.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Xiang Fu 香附

results.title_inferred: Chuan Lian Zi (Melia Fruit): Classical Properties, Actions, and Kampo Applications
results.original_text: 川楝子 — 苦，寒，有小毒。歸肝、小腸、膀胱經。疏肝泄熱，行氣止痛，殺蟲。主溫疾、傷寒大熱煩狂，殺三蟲、疥瘡、利小便水道。
results.translation:
Chuan Lian Zi [川楝子, 'Sichuan Pagoda-tree Fruit' / Melia fruit] — Flavor [wei]: bitter [ku 苦]. Nature [qi]: cold [han 寒]. Toxicity: mildly toxic [you xiao du 有小毒]. Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: enters the Liver [gan 肝], Small Intestine [xiao chang 小腸], and Urinary Bladder [pang guang 膀胱] channels.

Therapeutic actions [zhu zhi 主治]: Disperses Liver qi and drains Heat [shu gan xie re 疏肝泄熱]; moves qi and alleviates pain [xing qi zhi tong 行氣止痛]; kills parasites [sha chong 殺蟲]. Classically indicated for febrile disease [wen ji 溫疾] and the severe Heat, agitation, and manic states of Cold Damage [shang han 傷寒] disorders. Eliminates the three categories of intestinal parasites [sha san chong 殺三蟲]; treats scabies and sores [jie chuang 疥瘡]; promotes urination and opens the water passages [li xiao bian shui dao 利小便水道].

Classical Category: Middle Herb [zhong pin 中品] — substances possessing both therapeutic virtue and some degree of toxicity, to be used with appropriate caution and dosage regulation.

Japanese Kampo Name: Senrenshi [川楝子, せんれんし]. In the Kampo tradition, Chuan Lian Zi is principally valued as a Liver-coursing, qi-moving, and pain-relieving agent, especially for hypochondriac and epigastric pain attributed to Liver qi constraint with depressive Heat [gan yu hua re 肝鬱化熱]. It is also employed in antiparasitic formulations.

Key Classical and Kampo Formulas: (1) Jin Ling Zi San [金鈴子散, 'Melia Fruit Powder'] — combines Chuan Lian Zi with Yan Hu Suo [延胡索, Corydalis rhizome] to move qi, invigorate Blood, and relieve pain; indicated for hypochondriac pain, epigastric pain, and hernial pain [shan tong 疝痛] with signs of Heat. (2) Dao Qi Tang [導氣湯] — addresses cold-type hernial pain, often combined with warming herbs to moderate the cold nature of Chuan Lian Zi. (3) Various antiparasitic (qu chong 驅蟲) preparations in both Chinese and Kampo practice employ the fruit or its bark (ku lian pi 苦楝皮) against roundworms and other intestinal parasites.

Preparation notes: Typically used as dried ripe fruit (chao yong 炒用 — dry-fried to moderate toxicity and cold nature is preferred in many clinical contexts). Standard decoction dose: 4.5–9 g. External application as a paste or powder is recorded for parasitic skin conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 川楝子 Chuan Lian Zi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Melia toosendan Sieb. et Zucc. (Sichuan Chinaberry / Sichuan Pagoda Tree), family Meliaceae — dried ripe fruit. Distinct from the more widely distributed Melia azedarach, though both are used medicinally and sometimes conflated in historical texts.
entities_mentioned.notes: The fruit (Fructus Toosendan) is the standard medicinal part. The root bark (ku lian pi 苦楝皮) is a related but distinct drug used primarily as an antiparasitic. Active constituents include toosendanin and various limonoid triterpenoids responsible for both antiparasitic and toxic properties. Mildly toxic; overdose can cause hepatotoxicity and gastrointestinal disturbance. Use with caution in patients with Spleen-Stomach deficiency Cold.

entities_mentioned.name: 金鈴子散 Jin Ling Zi San
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula: Melia Fruit Powder. Source: Suwen Bingji Qi Yi Bao Ming Ji (素問病機氣宜保命集, c. 12th century CE, Liu Wansu).
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most prominent formulas featuring Chuan Lian Zi; foundational in the treatment of Liver constraint Heat pattern pain in both Chinese medicine and Kampo.

entities_mentioned.name: 延胡索 Yan Hu Suo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corydalis yanhusuo W.T. Wang, family Papaveraceae — rhizome.
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Chuan Lian Zi in Jin Ling Zi San; classified as a Blood-invigorating, qi-moving, analgesic herb. Contains tetrahydropalmatine with documented analgesic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: 三蟲 San Chong
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The 'three worms' — a classical collective term for intestinal parasites, variously interpreted as roundworms (Ascaris lumbricoides), pinworms (Enterobius vermicularis), and tapeworms (Taenia spp.).
entities_mentioned.notes: The exact species encompassed by 'san chong' varies across classical sources. Toosendanin in Melia fruit has demonstrated anthelmintic activity against Ascaris in modern pharmacological research.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a brief keyword-format stub rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage. This entry has therefore been reconstructed and synthesized from the attested Shennong Bencaojing tradition, the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596), and standard Kampo reference materials, following the conventions of Plantacopia's scholarly archive format. Where precise classical wording is uncertain, the translation represents the consensus of the received textual tradition rather than a single manuscript witness. The attribution of Chuan Lian Zi to the 'middle herb' [zhong pin] category reflects post-Han elaboration; the Shennong Bencaojing itself (c. 200 CE) does not explicitly list this drug in surviving canonical recensions, though it enters the mainstream materia medica tradition by the time of the Mingyi Bielu (名醫別錄, c. 3rd–5th century CE). Scholars should treat classical formula attributions as reflecting the broader tradition rather than the Shennong Bencaojing text strictly dated to c. 200 CE.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Chuan Lian Zi 川楝子

results.title_inferred: Yan Hu Suo (Corydalis Rhizome): Classical Entry on Blood-Stasis Pain
results.original_text: 延胡索，味辛，溫。主破血，產後諸病，因血所為者；婦人月經不調，腹中結塊，崩中淋露，產後血暈，暴血衝上，因損下血，或酒摩及煮服。
results.translation:
Yan Hu Suo [延胡索, Corydalis rhizome]. Flavor: acrid [xin 辛]. Nature: warm [wen 溫]. Primary actions: breaks up [static] blood [po xue 破血]; addresses the full range of post-partum disorders arising from blood [pathology]; regulates irregular menstruation in women; resolves abdominal masses and accumulations [jie kuai 結塊]; treats flooding and spotting [beng zhong lin lu 崩中淋露, i.e., heavy uterine bleeding and persistent vaginal discharge]; counteracts post-partum blood dizziness [xue yun 血暈]; and arrests sudden upward surging of blood [bao xue chong shang 暴血衝上] as well as bleeding below due to trauma [sun xia xue 損下血]. Preparation: ground with wine [jiu mo 酒摩] or decocted and taken orally [zhu fu 煮服].

— Japanese Kampo elaboration —
In the Kampo tradition, Yan Hu Suo (J: Engosaku 延胡索) is classified as a Middle-Grade herb [chūhin 中品] and is held to move qi [ri qi 理氣] as well as invigorate blood [huo xue 活血], rendering it the foremost single herb for pain [zhi tong yao 止痛藥] arising from blood stasis combined with qi stagnation. Kampo physicians emphasize that its analgesic efficacy spans the entire body: chest pain, epigastric pain, flank pain, dysmenorrhea, and traumatic injury pain are all within its compass. Channel tropism [귀경 / ki-kei 帰経]: Heart [xin], Liver [gan], Spleen [pi], and Lung [fei] channels. The wine-frying preparation [jiu chao 酒炒] is preferred in Kampo for enhancing blood-activating and analgesic effects; raw or vinegar-processed forms [cu zhi 醋製] are also employed, the latter accentuating liver-channel entry and pain relief in the lower abdomen.

Key classical and Kampo formulas:
• Yan Hu Suo San (延胡索散) — core formula for dysmenorrhea and menstrual irregularity due to blood stasis and qi stagnation.
• Jin Ling Zi San (金鈴子散, J: Kinreishi-san) — pairs Yan Hu Suo with Chuan Lian Zi [Melia toosendan] for liver-qi stagnation with heat manifesting as chest, epigastric, or flank pain; one of the most celebrated pain formulas in the Sino-Japanese tradition.
• Ge Xia Zhu Yu Tang (膈下逐瘀湯) — employs Yan Hu Suo within a larger blood-stasis-dispelling formula for sub-diaphragmatic masses and pain.
• An Zhong San (安中散, J: Anchūsan) — widely used in Japanese Kampo for epigastric pain, cold-type gastralgia, and functional dyspepsia; Yan Hu Suo is a principal analgesic component.

entities_mentioned.name: 延胡索 Yan Hu Suo / Engosaku (延胡索)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corydalis yanhusuo W.T.Wang (family Papaveraceae / Fumariaceae); rhizome used medicinally.
