Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition and Kampo elaboration): 
1. Invigorates blood [huo xue] and unblocks the collaterals [tong luo]: Used for blood stasis [xue yu] manifesting as irregular menstruation, amenorrhea, and fixed abdominal masses [zheng jia]. 
2. Dispels wind-dampness [qu feng chu shi]: Addresses painful obstruction syndrome [bi zheng] — joint pain, numbness of the extremities, and stiffness of the sinews caused by wind-cold-damp invasion. 
3. Promotes water metabolism and reduces swelling [li shui xiao zhong]: Treats edema and difficult urination secondary to qi and blood stagnation. 
4. Promotes lactation [tong ru]: Classically indicated for insufficient lactation due to obstructed channels, often combined with Wang Bu Liu Xing (Vaccaria seed) and Mu Tong. 

Japanese Kampo Name: Ro-ro-tsu (路路通) — used in Kampo primarily as a supporting herb in formulas addressing wind-damp painful obstruction [fu-shu-hi-sho] and blood stasis with channel blockage [ketsu-o-rakusetsu]. Kampo texts (post-Edo elaborations) note its dispersing [san] character and classify it among herbs that 'open all orifices and passages.' 

Classical Category: Middle herb [zhong pin] — possesses both therapeutic benefit and mild potential for overconsumption; not listed explicitly in the original Shennong Bencaojing canon (c. 200 CE), but enters the materia medica tradition through the Bencao Gangmu (Li Shizhen, 1596) and subsequent elaborations, where it is cross-referenced to feng xiang zhi [楓香脂, Liquidambar resin], which does appear in earlier texts. 

Five Flavors [wu wei]: Bitter [ku], with a secondary astringent quality [se]. 
Four Natures [si qi]: Neutral [ping], tending mildly toward warmth [wei wen] in Kampo interpretation. 
Channel Tropism [gui jing]: Liver [gan jing], Kidney [shen jing]; secondary: Stomach [wei jing]. 

Key Classical Formulas: 
— Lu Lu Tong is most frequently employed as an adjunct rather than a chief herb [jun]. It appears in modified versions of Juan Bi Tang [蠲痹湯] for wind-damp bi syndrome; in lactation-promoting decoctions alongside Wang Bu Liu Xing and Chuan Shan Jia; and in blood-invigorating formulas modeled on Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang [血府逐瘀湯] for gynecological stasis. 

Contraindications: Contraindicated in pregnancy [yun fu jin yong]. Use with caution in patients with deficiency without stasis, as its dispersing nature may injure righteous qi [zheng qi].

entities_mentioned.name: Lu Lu Tong 路路通
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquidambar taiwaniana Hance (Formosan sweetgum) fruit capitulum; also Liquidambar orientalis Mill. or L. styraciflua in some regional traditions (family Hamamelidaceae / Altingiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried spiny spherical fruiting head (capitulum) is the medicinal part used. 'Lu Lu Tong' literally means 'all roads open,' reflecting its classical action of unblocking channels and collaterals. Not to be confused with Feng Xiang Zhi (楓香脂), the resinous exudate of the same tree, which has a distinct entry in earlier bencao texts and emphasizes external and topical applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Feng Xiang Zhi 楓香脂
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquidambar resin / storax-type exudate from Liquidambar spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The resin of the same Liquidambar tree appears in earlier classical texts including the Bencao Gangmu. It shares some channel-opening properties but is more often used topically for sores, bleeding, and pain. Lu Lu Tong (the fruit) was systematically distinguished from Feng Xiang Zhi in Qing-dynasty bencao scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: Wang Bu Liu Xing 王不留行
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vaccaria hispanica (Mill.) Rauschert seed (Caryophyllaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently combined with Lu Lu Tong in lactation-promoting formulas; both herbs share the action of unblocking channels and promoting the downward movement of milk.

entities_mentioned.name: Juan Bi Tang 蠲痹湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula for wind-cold-damp painful obstruction [bi zheng]; Lu Lu Tong is added in modified versions to enhance collateral-unblocking action.

entities_mentioned.name: Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang 血府逐瘀湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical blood-invigorating formula attributed to Wang Qingren (Yilin Gaicuo, 1830); Lu Lu Tong may be added as a modification for obstruction of the lower jiao or extremities.

results.translator_notes: Lu Lu Tong does not appear by name in the original Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE), which predates its systematic entry into the Chinese materia medica canon. Its full codification occurs in the Bencao Gangmu Shiyi (趙學敏, 1765) and related Qing-dynasty compilations. The entry above synthesizes the Shennong Bencaojing tradition (via Feng Xiang Zhi, the related resin entry) with the later elaborated monograph and its Kampo reception in Edo- and Meiji-period Japanese herbal medicine. Kampo usage is attested in texts such as the Koho-ha (古方派) and Gosei-ha (後世派) schools, where the herb is valued primarily for bi syndrome and blood stasis rather than as a standalone primary herb. The source segment was extremely terse; this entry has been reconstructed from the full classical and Kampo textual tradition consistent with the Plantacopia mandate. Confidence is high for pharmacological identity and channel tropism; the 'middle herb' classification is an inference, as classical rank is not assigned to post-Bencaojing additions.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Lu Lu Tong 路路通

results.title_inferred: Chuan Bei Mu (Sichuan Fritillary Bulb): Heat-Phlegm, Lung, and Cough
results.original_text: 川貝母 — 味苦甘，性微寒。歸肺、心經。清熱潤肺，化痰止咳，散結消癰。主治肺熱燥咳，乾咳少痰，陰虛勞嗽，痰中帶血，瘰癧，乳癰，肺癰。
results.translation:
Chuan Bei Mu [Sichuan Fritillary Bulb; 川貝母]. 

**Chinese Name:** Chuān Bèi Mǔ (川貝母). **Japanese Kampo Name:** Senbaimu (川貝母). **Classical Category:** Middle-grade herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition — herbs of this grade are used therapeutically but must be employed with care regarding dose and duration.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Bitter [苦, kǔ] and Sweet [甘, gān].

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Slightly Cold [微寒, wēi hán].

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Lung [肺, Fèi] and Heart [心, Xīn] channels.

**Therapeutic Actions:** (1) Clears Heat and Moistens the Lung [清熱潤肺, qīng rè rùn fèi]: indicated for Lung-Heat dryness-cough [肺熱燥咳], dry cough with scant phlegm, and Yin-deficiency taxation-cough [陰虛勞嗽] with blood-streaked sputum. (2) Transforms Phlegm and Arrests Cough [化痰止咳, huà tán zhǐ ké]: dissolves stubborn heat-phlegm lodged in the Lung; particularly suited to phlegm of a viscous, difficult-to-expectorate character arising from Heat or dryness, distinguishing it from the more pungently dispersing Zhe Bei Mu [浙貝母]. (3) Dissipates Nodules and Resolves Abscess [散結消癰, sàn jié xiāo yōng]: used for scrofula [瘰癧, luǒ lì], breast abscess [乳癰, rǔ yōng], and Lung abscess [肺癰, fèi yōng].

**Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:** (a) *Chuanbei Pipa Gao* [川貝枇杷膏] — a honey-based syrup pairing Chuan Bei Mu with Pipa Ye [枇杷葉, loquat leaf] for Lung-Heat dry cough; widely used in both Chinese and Kampo traditions. (b) *Erhmu San* [二母散] — combines Bei Mu with Zhi Mu [知母, Anemarrhena] to clear Lung and Kidney deficiency-fire with cough. (c) *Beimu Gualou San* [貝母瓜蔞散] — pairs Bei Mu with Gualou [瓜蔞, trichosanthes fruit] to moisten dryness and transform phlegm; a key formula in Kampo for dry, unproductive cough. (d) In Kampo elaboration, Senbaimu frequently appears in *ninjin yoei-to* variant compounds addressing debility-cough with Yin vacuity.

**Contraindications and Cautions:** Incompatible with Wu Tou [烏頭, aconite] — this pairing constitutes one of the classical 'Eighteen Antagonisms' [十八反, shí bā fǎn]. Not appropriate for cough arising from Wind-Cold or Cold-Phlegm, where warming, dispersing agents are required instead.

entities_mentioned.name: Chuan Bei Mu 川貝母
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fritillaria cirrhosa D. Don (and related Fritillaria species: F. unibracteata, F. przewalskii, F. delavayi — family Liliaceae). The dried bulb is used medicinally.
entities_mentioned.notes: Chuan Bei Mu ('Sichuan Fritillary') is distinguished in modern Chinese Pharmacopoeia from Zhe Bei Mu (Fritillaria thunbergii), the Zhejiang variety. Chuan Bei Mu is smaller, sweeter, and milder — preferred for deficiency-pattern coughs with dryness and Yin vacuity. Zhe Bei Mu is larger, more bitter, and more powerfully dissipating — preferred for excess heat-phlegm and nodules.

entities_mentioned.name: Zhe Bei Mu 浙貝母
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fritillaria thunbergii Miq. (family Liliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a comparative herb to clarify the distinct therapeutic profile of Chuan Bei Mu. Classified as more bitter and cold, suited to excess-pattern phlegm-heat rather than deficiency-dryness.

entities_mentioned.name: Pipa Ye 枇杷葉
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eriobotrya japonica (Thunb.) Lindl. — loquat leaf (family Rosaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly paired with Chuan Bei Mu in classical and patent formulas for Lung-Heat cough.

entities_mentioned.name: Zhi Mu 知母
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anemarrhena asphodeloides Bunge (family Asparagaceae / formerly Liliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Bei Mu in Erhmu San to address deficiency-fire with cough and consumptive conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Gualou 瓜蔞
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trichosanthes kirilowii Maxim. — fruit (family Cucurbitaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Key pairing in Beimu Gualou San for phlegm-dryness cough.

entities_mentioned.name: Wu Tou 烏頭
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux — processed root (family Ranunculaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as an antagonist to Bei Mu in the classical Eighteen Antagonisms [十八反]. Combined use is contraindicated in classical and contemporary Chinese and Kampo practice.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a brief keyword descriptor rather than a verbatim classical passage; accordingly, this entry has been reconstructed from the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, the Bencao Gangmu (李時珍, 1596 CE), and subsequent Kampo (Japanese) elaborations, following standard Plantacopia methodology for herb-entry generation from classical corpora. The original_text field presents a normative classical Chinese summary consistent with the received Bencao tradition rather than a single verbatim quotation, since no verbatim primary-source passage was supplied. Scholars should consult Bencao Gangmu Juan 13 and the Japanese Kampo reference Honzo Wamyo and Yakucho for primary attestations. The distinction between Chuan Bei Mu and Zhe Bei Mu is a post-Song dynasty elaboration; the Shennong Bencaojing itself lists Bei Mu [貝母] without the Chuan/Zhe geographic qualifier.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Chuan Bei Mu 川貝母

results.title_inferred: Zhe Bei Mu (Thunberg Fritillary Bulb): Phlegm-Heat and Scrofulous Masses
results.original_text: 浙貝母 — Fritillaria thunbergii — 痰熱，癥瘕積聚
results.translation:
Zhe Bei Mu (浙貝母, pinyin: zhè bèi mǔ; Japanese Kampo: Setsuhimo / 浙貝母, also rendered Beimu 貝母 in older Kampo texts where the Zhejiang and Sichuan varieties were not always distinguished). 

Classical category: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing taxonomy — herbs of this class supplement deficiency and correct imbalance, but require careful dosing and are not for indefinite use. 

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Bitter [苦, kǔ]. 

Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Cold [寒, hán]. 

Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Lung [肺, fèi]; Heart [心, xīn]; in later elaborations (Jin-Yuan through Qing dynasty commentators) also attributed to the Stomach channel [胃, wèi]. 

Therapeutic Actions: 
(1) Clears Heat and Transforms Phlegm [清熱化痰, qīng rè huà tán]: Indicated for cough with thick, yellow, or difficult-to-expectorate phlegm arising from Lung heat or phlegm-heat obstruction. Distinguished from Chuan Bei Mu (川貝母, Fritillaria cirrhosa) in that Zhe Bei Mu is held to be more powerfully bitter and cold, making it better suited for excess [實, shí] phlegm-heat patterns rather than the deficiency-dryness patterns for which Chuan Bei Mu is preferred. 
(2) Disperses Nodules and Dissipates Masses [散結消癰, sàn jié xiāo yōng]: Classically indicated for scrofulous lumps [瘰癧, luǒ lì], goiter [癭瘤, yǐng liú], breast abscesses [乳癰, rǔ yōng], lung abscesses [肺癰, fèi yōng], and intestinal abscesses [腸癰, cháng yōng]. The bitter-cold nature is understood to resolve phlegm-fire stagnation that gives rise to hard masses. 
(3) Relieves Toxicity and Reduces Swelling [解毒消腫, jiě dú xiāo zhǒng]: Used topically and internally for swellings with heat signs. 

Key Classical and Kampo Formulas: 
— Xiao Luo Wan (消瘰丸): Zhe Bei Mu paired with Xuan Shen (玄參, Scrophularia) and Mu Li (牡蠣, oyster shell) for phlegm-fire scrofula; a canonical formula from the Qing dynasty Yi Xue Xin Wu (醫學心悟). 
— Fritillaria and Trichosanthes Formula (貝母瓜蔞散, Bèimǔ Guālóu Sǎn): Zhe Bei Mu combined with Gua Lou (瓜蔞), Tian Hua Fen (天花粉), Fu Ling (茯苓), Chen Pi (陳皮), and Jie Geng (桔梗) for phlegm-heat cough with difficult expectoration. 
— In Kampo practice, Beimu appears in formulations addressing 咳嗽 (kasō, cough) with heat signs and 頸部リンパ節腫脹 (cervical lymphadenopathy), though Kampo texts often specify the Sichuan variety for moistening and Zhe Bei Mu for dispersing hard nodules. 

Contraindications and Cautions [配伍禁忌, pèi wǔ jìnjì]: Traditionally listed among the herbs that antagonize [反, fǎn] Wu Tou (烏頭, Aconitum) according to the classical 'Eighteen Incompatibilities' [十八反, shí bā fǎn]. Not appropriate for cold-phlegm patterns or Spleen-deficiency presentations.

entities_mentioned.name: 浙貝母 (Zhe Bei Mu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fritillaria thunbergii Miq. (Thunberg's fritillary), family Liliaceae; bulb used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Chuan Bei Mu (川貝母, Fritillaria cirrhosa and allied spp.) in both botany and clinical emphasis. The Zhejiang variety became separately codified in materia medica texts from the Ming dynasty onward; earlier Shennong Bencaojing references to 貝母 (Bèi mǔ) may conflate the two taxa.

entities_mentioned.name: 貝母 (Bei Mu, undifferentiated)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fritillaria spp. collectively (F. thunbergii, F. cirrhosa, F. ussuriensis, etc.)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Shennong Bencaojing entry uses the undifferentiated term 貝母; the Zhe/Chuan distinction is a post-classical refinement formalized fully by the Qing period.

entities_mentioned.name: Xiao Luo Wan (消瘰丸)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula from Cheng Guopeng's Yi Xue Xin Wu (醫學心悟, 1732 CE); not a Shennong Bencaojing formula but a key later formulation in which Zhe Bei Mu is the principal herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Beimu Gualou San (貝母瓜蔞散)
entities_mentioned.notes: Phlegm-heat cough formula; Qing dynasty provenance. Relevant to both Chinese medicine and Japanese Kampo practice.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a brief botanical–clinical stub rather than a verbatim classical passage, so this entry has been reconstructed from the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, Ming–Qing materia medica elaborations (principally Bencao Gangmu 本草綱目 and Bencao Zhengyi 本草正義), and standard Kampo source material. No verbatim classical Chinese passage was provided; the 'original_text' field preserves the supplied segment label and key Chinese characters. The Shennong Bencaojing's own entry uses the undifferentiated term 貝母 (Bei Mu) and the Zhejiang/Sichuan distinction post-dates the classic by many centuries — this is flagged explicitly in the translation and entity notes to preserve scholarly accuracy. The 'Eighteen Incompatibilities' warning regarding Aconitum is a standard classical contraindication that should be noted for any clinical or historical discussion.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Zhe Bei Mu 浙貝母

results.title_inferred: Gua Lou (Trichosanthes Fruit): Classical Materia Medica Entry on Phlegm-Heat, Chest Obstruction, and Constipation
results.original_text: 瓜蔞 (Guā Lóu) — 栝樓 (alt. graph: Guā Lóu) — 天花粉 (Tiān Huā Fěn, for root). 《神農本草經》中品. 味苦，寒. 主消渴，身熱，煩滿，大熱，補虛安中，續絕傷. 一名地樓. 生川谷及山陰.
results.translation:
Gua Lou [瓜蔞, Trichosanthes kirilowii Maxim. and related species] — also written 栝樓; the dried fruit (Fructus Trichosanthis) is Gua Lou, while the root yields Tian Hua Fen [天花粉, 'Heavenly Flower Powder']. Alternate classical name: Di Lou [地樓, 'Ground Trellis']. Grows in river valleys and on the shaded slopes of mountains.

[CLASSICAL CATEGORY — Shennong Bencaojing]: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — medicines of the Middle rank tonify and supplement; they may be used over extended periods with appropriate caution.

[FIVE FLAVORS — wǔ wèi]: Bitter [苦, kǔ]; some later authorities (Li Shizhen, Bencao Gangmu, 1596) add Sweet [甘, gān] for the fruit peel (Gua Lou Pi) and note a slight Cold-Slippery quality in the seeds (Gua Lou Ren).

[FOUR NATURES — sì qì]: Cold [寒, hán].

[CHANNEL TROPISM — guī jīng]: Lung [肺], Stomach [胃], Large Intestine [大腸]. (Tropism formalized in Jin-Yuan and later Kampo elaboration; implied in classical indications.)

[THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS — Shennong Bencaojing tradition]: Governs [主] wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě — akin to diabetic or febrile fluid-wasting syndromes], bodily heat [身熱], vexation and fullness [煩滿, fán mǎn], great heat [大熱]. Supplements vacuity and quiets the center [補虛安中]. Reconnects severed injury [續絕傷 — promotes healing of traumatic rupture or sinew damage].

[EXPANDED KAMPO AND POST-CLASSICAL ACTIONS]: (1) Clears Heat and transforms Phlegm [清熱化痰, qīng rè huà tán]: for cough with thick, difficult-to-expectorate yellow phlegm due to Phlegm-Heat obstructing the Lung. (2) Loosens the chest and dissipates binding [寬胸散結, kuān xiōng sàn jié]: for chest bi-obstruction [胸痹, xiōng bì] — oppression, pain, and tightness of the chest associated with Phlegm and Heat constraining the chest yang. (3) Moistens the Intestines and frees the stool [潤腸通便, rùn cháng tōng biàn]: for constipation due to Intestinal dryness, especially when accompanied by Heat. (4) Disperses swellings and expels pus [消腫排膿]: topically or internally for early-stage Lung abscess [肺癰], breast abscess [乳癰], and intestinal abscess [腸癰].

[PARTS USED AND PREPARATION]: The whole ripe fruit (Gua Lou, 瓜蔞) may be used entire; the peel (Gua Lou Pi, 瓜蔞皮) emphasizes chest-loosening and phlegm-transforming actions; the seeds (Gua Lou Ren, 瓜蔞仁) emphasize moistening and laxative actions; the root (Tian Hua Fen, 天花粉) clears Heat, generates fluids, and reduces swelling. Decoction [煎服] is standard; seeds may be ground. Dose in classical usage: 9–20 g (whole fruit); 10–15 g (root powder).

[KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS — Kampo and Shanghanlun tradition]: (1) Gua Lou Xie Bai Bai Jiu Tang [栝樓薤白白酒湯] (Jingui Yaolüe, Zhang Zhongjing, c.220 CE) — Gua Lou, Xie Bai [薤白], Bai Jiu [白酒 (rice wine)]: primary formula for chest bi-obstruction with phlegm-cold; (2) Gua Lou Xie Bai Ban Xia Tang [栝樓薤白半夏湯] — adds Ban Xia [半夏] for phlegm-cold with more severe obstruction; (3) Xiao Xian Xiong Tang [小陷胸湯] (Shanghanlun) — Gua Lou, Huang Lian [黃連], Ban Xia: clears Phlegm-Heat from the chest (minor chest bind [小結胸]); (4) Gua Lou Niu Bang Tang [瓜蔞牛蒡湯] (Kampo elaboration) — for breast abscess and throat swelling. CONTRAINDICATION: Incompatible [相反] with Wu Tou [烏頭, Aconite] in classical drug-interaction theory (十八反, Eighteen Incompatibilities).

entities_mentioned.name: 瓜蔞 / 栝樓 (Gua Lou)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trichosanthes kirilowii Maxim. (Chinese: 栝樓); also T. rosthornii Harms — family Cucurbitaceae. Fruit = Fructus Trichosanthis; Root = Radix Trichosanthis (Tian Hua Fen).
entities_mentioned.notes: The alternate graph 栝樓 is the older, more classical form; 瓜蔞 is the simplified/post-Tang conventional form. Both refer to the same plant. The Japanese Kampo name is カロコン (Karokon) for the root (天花粉) and カロウ (Karō) or 栝楼 (Karō) for the fruit. The root, Tian Hua Fen, achieved independent materia medica status by the Tang dynasty (Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE).

entities_mentioned.name: 天花粉 (Tian Hua Fen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Radix Trichosanthis — the dried root of Trichosanthes kirilowii or T. rosthornii.
entities_mentioned.notes: In modern TCM and Kampo, Tian Hua Fen is listed separately from Gua Lou fruit and carries distinct indications (fluid generation, Heat clearance, abscess reduction). It contains trichosanthin, a ribosome-inactivating protein investigated in 20th-century antiviral research.

entities_mentioned.name: 地樓 (Di Lou)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternate classical name for Trichosanthes (same plant).
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in Shennong Bencaojing as the 'one name' (一名) variant; literally 'ground trellis,' referencing the plant's climbing habit.

entities_mentioned.name: 小陷胸湯 (Xiao Xian Xiong Tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula from Shanghanlun (Zhang Zhongjing, c.220 CE): Trichosanthes fruit + Coptis chinensis + Pinellia ternata.
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most cited Gua Lou formulas in both Chinese and Japanese Kampo practice; used for 'minor chest bind' pattern (小結胸證) with epigastric and chest fullness, tenderness, and yellow phlegm.

entities_mentioned.name: 栝樓薤白白酒湯 (Gua Lou Xie Bai Bai Jiu Tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula from Jingui Yaolüe (Zhang Zhongjing): Trichosanthes fruit + Allium macrostemon (Xie Bai) + rice wine.
entities_mentioned.notes: Foundational formula for chest bi-obstruction (胸痹); widely used in Kampo cardiology. Xie Bai = Bulbus Allii Macrostemi.

entities_mentioned.name: 烏頭 (Wu Tou)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux — Aconite root (processed: Zhi Chuan Wu / Zhi Fu Zi).
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical incompatibility (十八反): Gua Lou is held to be antagonistic to all forms of Wu Tou/Fu Zi. This pairing is contraindicated in both classical Chinese and Kampo formulary traditions, though the precise pharmacological basis remains debated.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment is a brief modern scholarly label rather than a verbatim classical passage; the original_text field reconstructs the core classical Chinese wording from standard Shennong Bencaojing critical editions (e.g., Sun Xingyan collation, Qing dynasty) to provide an authentic classical anchor for the entry. The expanded therapeutic and formula content is drawn from the Shanghanlun, Jingui Yaolüe, Bencao Gangmu (李時珍, 1596), and standard Kampo elaborations (Yakuchū, 薬徴 Yakucho by Yoshimasu Todo, 1762). 2. The Five Flavors classification 'bitter' originates in the Shennong Bencaojing; 'sweet' for the fruit peel is a later differentiation found in Song and Ming sources. Both are noted for completeness. 3. Channel tropism (gui jing) is not explicit in the original Shennong Bencaojing but is a Jin-Yuan dynasty systematization (c.1100–1368 CE) that became standard in Kampo theory; it is included as contextually essential. 4. The Kampo Japanese reading of 栝楼 is カロウ (Karō); the root 天花粉 is テンカフン (Tenkafun). Both appear in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (日本薬局方). 5. Trichosanthin (天花粉蛋白) from the root attracted significant biomedical attention in the late 20th century for abortifacient and antiviral properties — this is noted for historical completeness but lies outside the classical period scope of the entry.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Gua Lou 瓜蔞

results.title_inferred: Ban Xia (Half-Summer Root): Pinellia for Cold Phlegm, Nausea, and Abdominal Masses
results.original_text: 半夏，味辛，平。主傷寒，寒熱，心下堅，下氣，喉咽腫痛，頭眩，胸脹，咳逆，腸鳴，止汗。一名地文，一名水玉。生川谷。
results.translation:
Ban Xia [半夏, 'Half-Summer']: Flavor is acrid [xin 辛]; nature is neutral/balanced [ping 平]. It governs: cold-damage [shang han 傷寒] disorders; alternating cold and heat; hardness and fullness below the heart [xin xia jian 心下堅]; descending of counterflow qi [xia qi 下氣]; swollen and painful throat; dizziness and vertigo [tou xuan 頭眩]; chest distension [xiong zhang 胸脹]; coughing with counterflow [ke ni 咳逆]; intestinal rumbling [chang ming 腸鳴]; and it arrests sweating [zhi han 止汗]. Alternate names: Di Wen [地文, 'Ground Inscription'] and Shui Yu [水玉, 'Water Jade']. It grows in river valleys and gorges [chuan gu 川谷].

— CLASSICAL CATEGORY (Shennong Bencaojing): Lower/Inferior Herb [xia pin 下品]. Herbs of this class are typically potent and may exhibit toxicity; they are used therapeutically to address established disease rather than for long-term tonic use. Ban Xia is explicitly noted in early commentaries as toxic [you du 有毒], requiring preparation to mitigate its harsh properties.

— FIVE FLAVORS [wu wei 五味]: Acrid [xin 辛]. Acrid flavor governs dispersion, movement, and the promotion of qi and fluid circulation. It facilitates the breaking up of accumulated phlegm and the diffusion of lung qi.

— FOUR NATURES [si qi 四氣]: Warm [wen 溫] — Note: The Shennong Bencaojing records ping (neutral/balanced), but the dominant later tradition, including the Mingyi Bielu (名醫別錄) and standard Kampo classification, reassigns Ban Xia to warm [wen]. This is the clinically operative classification and reflects its action of transforming cold-damp phlegm [han tan 寒痰].

— CHANNEL TROPISM [gui jing 歸經] (post-classical elaboration, Tang–Song onward): Spleen [pi 脾], Stomach [wei 胃], Lung [fei 肺]. This reflects Ban Xia's primary clinical sphere: drying dampness in the middle burner [zhong jiao 中焦], harmonizing the stomach to arrest nausea and vomiting, and transforming phlegm-damp obstructing the lung.

— THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (synthesized from Shennong Bencaojing, Mingyi Bielu, and Kampo elaborations):
  1. Dries dampness and transforms phlegm [zao shi hua tan 燥濕化痰]: Ban Xia is the foremost herb for cold-damp and cold phlegm conditions, especially when phlegm obstructs the lung or accumulates in the middle burner, causing cough with copious white or clear sputum, chest oppression, and wheezing.
  2. Descends counterflow qi and arrests vomiting [jiang ni zhi ou 降逆止嘔]: It is the paramount herb for nausea and vomiting in the classical tradition, particularly when caused by cold-phlegm or stomach cold. It harmonizes the stomach and redirects rebellious stomach qi downward.
  3. Disperses masses and dissipates nodules [san jie xiao pi 散結消痞]: Ban Xia addresses focal distension [pi 痞], epigastric hardness, and accumulations arising from phlegm and qi stagnation. Relevant to the classical concept of 'plum-pit qi' [mei he qi 梅核氣] — the sensation of obstruction in the throat — and scrofula [luo li 瘰癧].
  4. Reduces swelling and alleviates pain (external use): Classically applied topically (raw form) for swellings, carbuncles, and snake-bite, though this application requires caution given its toxicity.

— TOXICITY AND PREPARATION: Raw Ban Xia [sheng ban xia 生半夏] is considered toxic [you du 有毒], causing irritation of the oral mucosa and throat (numbness, swelling). Classical and Kampo practice mandates processing before internal use:
  • Fa Ban Xia [法半夏]: Processed with alum [bai fan 白礬] and/or ginger [jiang 薑]; strengthens phlegm-drying action.
  • Jiang Ban Xia [薑半夏]: Processed with fresh ginger [sheng jiang 生薑] and alum; enhances antiemetic action, reduces toxicity.
  • Qing Ban Xia [清半夏]: Processed with alum solution; emphasizes phlegm-transforming, drying action.
  In the Shang Han Lun tradition, Ban Xia is almost always used in conjunction with Sheng Jiang [生薑, fresh ginger], which both counteracts its toxicity and synergizes its antiemetic effect.

— JAPANESE KAMPO NAME: Hange [ハンゲ / 半夏]. Hange is one of the most frequently employed herbs in classical Kampo formulas. Kampo literature (especially Yoshimasu Tōdō's Yakuchō [藥徵], 1771) affirms its primary indication as 'treating counterflow and vomiting' and 'sensation of something stuck in the heart region or chest.'

— KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS:
  1. Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang [半夏瀉心湯] (Shang Han Lun): Ban Xia + Huang Qin + Huang Lian + Ren Shen + Gan Jiang + Da Zao + Zhi Gan Cao. Treats 'stomach duct focal distension' [xin xia pi 心下痞] with nausea, vomiting, and intestinal rumbling. Kampo: Hange Shashin-tō.
  2. Xiao Ban Xia Tang [小半夏湯] (Jingui Yaolue): Ban Xia + Sheng Jiang. The foundational antiemetic formula.
  3. Ban Xia Hou Po Tang [半夏厚朴湯] (Jingui Yaolue): Ban Xia + Hou Po + Fu Ling + Sheng Jiang + Zi Su Ye. Treats plum-pit qi [mei he qi] and phlegm-qi obstruction. Kampo: Hange Koboku-tō.
  4. Xiao Chai Hu Tang [小柴胡湯] (Shang Han Lun): Ban Xia is a core ingredient, harmonizing the lesser yang [shao yang 少陽] stage; addresses alternating chills and fever, nausea, and fullness below the ribs. Kampo: Shō Saiko-tō.
  5. Er Chen Tang [二陳湯] (Taiping Huimin Heji Jufang, Song dynasty): The foundational phlegm-damp formula; Ban Xia + Chen Pi + Fu Ling + Zhi Gan Cao.

— CONTRAINDICATIONS (classical): Contraindicated in cases of yin deficiency with dry cough, bleeding disorders, and during pregnancy (classical caution). Incompatible [xiang fan 相反] with Wu Tou [烏頭] (aconite root) according to the classical eighteen incompatibilities [shi ba fan 十八反].

entities_mentioned.name: Ban Xia 半夏
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino — dried rhizome (Araceae family)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most firmly and consistently identified herbs in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition. The alternate classical names Di Wen (地文) and Shui Yu (水玉) are well attested. Grows in moist, partially shaded habitats across China, Japan, and Korea. Rhizome harvested in summer and autumn. Raw form is toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals and other irritant alkaloids; processing is mandatory for internal use.

entities_mentioned.name: Hange ハンゲ / 半夏
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino
entities_mentioned.notes: Japanese Kampo name directly adopted from Chinese. Pharmacopoeial standard in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP). Central to numerous classical Kampo formulas, particularly those in the Shang Han Lun and Jingui Yaolue traditions as transmitted and elaborated in Japan from the 7th century onward.

entities_mentioned.name: Wu Tou 烏頭
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux (or related Aconitum spp.) — root
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as classically incompatible (xiang fan) with Ban Xia in the eighteen incompatibilities schema. Both herbs are potent; their combination is prohibited in standard practice, though there is ongoing scholarly debate about the empirical basis of this prohibition.

results.translator_notes:
1. NATURE DISCREPANCY: The Shennong Bencaojing records Ban Xia's nature as ping (平, neutral/balanced). The later and now-standard classification shifts this to wen (溫, warm), a revision first consolidated in the Mingyi Bielu (名醫別錄, c. 3rd–5th c. CE) and carried into all major subsequent materia medica (Bencao Gangmu included). The entry preserves both readings with explanation, as the shift is clinically and historically significant. Kampo sources uniformly follow the warm classification.

2. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: The 'original_text' field renders the reconstructed Shennong Bencaojing entry for Ban Xia based on the standard Mori Tateyuki (森立之) and Sun Xingyan (孫星衍) critical editions (Qing dynasty reconstructions), as the original Shennong Bencaojing does not survive as an independent text. Minor variation exists across recensions.

3. TOXICITY VOCABULARY: The term you du (有毒, 'has toxicity') is a formal classical pharmacological category, not merely a colloquial warning. Herbs so classified in the Shennong Bencaojing system carry specific prescriptive implications regarding dosage, preparation, and combination.

4. YOSHIMASU TŌDŌ REFERENCE: The citation of Yakuchō (藥徵) reflects the dominant Kohō-ha (古方派, 'Classical Formula School') Kampo current, which emphasized direct return to the Shang Han Lun. This school's characterization of Ban Xia's indications is particularly influential in modern Kampo practice and differs in some emphases from the Chinese literati medicine tradition.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Ban Xia 半夏

results.title_inferred: Tian Nan Xing (天南星) — Jack-in-the-Pulpit Rhizome: Cold Phlegm, Wind-Stroke, and Seizure
results.original_text: 天南星，味苦辛，性溫，有毒。主治中風麻痺，除痰涎，攻堅積，治驚癇，散血墮胎。生山谷。
results.translation:
Tian Nan Xing [Heaven South Star] (天南星): flavor bitter and acrid [xin], nature warm [wen], toxic [you du]. It governs wind-strike with numbness and paralysis [zhong feng ma bi], eliminates phlegm-drool [tan xian], attacks hard accumulations [jian ji], treats fright-epilepsy [jing xian], disperses stagnant blood [san xue], and descends the fetus [duo tai]. It grows in mountain valleys.

— Classical Category (Shennong Bencaojing tradition): Inferior Herb [Xia Pin 下品], reflecting its toxicity and aggressive action.

— Five Flavors [Wu Wei 五味]: Bitter [ku 苦] and Acrid [xin 辛].

— Four Natures [Si Qi 四氣]: Warm [wen 溫]. Toxic [you du 有毒] — requires processing (bile-treatment or ginger-processing) to reduce toxicity before internal use.

— Channel Tropism [Gui Jing 歸經]: Lung [Fei 肺], Liver [Gan 肝], Spleen [Pi 脾]. The Lung governs phlegm transformation; the Liver governs wind and convulsion; the Spleen governs dampness and generation of phlegm.

— Therapeutic Actions (classical and Kampo elaboration):
  1. Dries Dampness and Transforms Phlegm [zao shi hua tan 燥濕化痰]: Addresses stubborn cold-phlegm accumulations in the chest, lungs, and channels; stronger drying action than Ban Xia (半夏).
  2. Dispels Wind and Stops Convulsions [qu feng zhi jing 祛風止痙]: Treats wind-phlegm obstructing the channels and orifices, manifesting as facial paralysis [kou yan wai xie 口眼歪斜], wind-stroke hemiplegia [zhong feng pian tan 中風偏癱], and tetanic spasm [jing feng 驚風].
  3. Disperses Swelling and Relieves Pain [san jie zhi tong 散結止痛]: Applied topically to abscesses, toxic swellings, and snake bites; the raw (unprocessed) form is preferred for external use.
  4. Calms Fright and Controls Epilepsy [zhen jing zhi xian 鎮驚止癇]: Classical indication for fright-epilepsy [jing xian], phlegm confounding the Heart orifice.

— Japanese Kampo Name: Tennanshō (天南星, テンナンショウ). Used in Kampo primarily in wind-phlegm conditions and in formulas targeting stubborn phlegm obstructing the channels. Processed with ox-bile to yield Tannansei (膽南星, tansōsei / dǎn nán xīng), which cools the nature and is preferred for heat-phlegm and convulsive disorders in children.

— Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:
  • Dao Tan Tang (導痰湯) — 'Guide Out Phlegm Decoction': Tian Nan Xing with Ban Xia, Chen Pi, Fu Ling, Zhi Shi, Gan Cao; treats stubborn phlegm-damp obstructing the channels with dizziness and numbness.
  • Qian Zheng San (牽正散) — 'Lead to Correctness Powder': paired with Bai Fu Zi and Quan Xie for facial paralysis due to wind-phlegm.
  • San Sheng Wan (三生丸) — uses raw (sheng) Tian Nan Xing with raw Chuan Wu and raw Fu Zi for severe cold-phlegm wind-stroke.
  • Dan Nan Xing (膽南星) formulas — bile-processed form used in pediatric convulsions and phlegm-heat seizure disorders in Kampo pediatric practice.

— Preparation Notes: Raw Tian Nan Xing is highly toxic and used almost exclusively externally. For internal use, it is processed: (a) ginger-processing [jiang zhi 薑制] — soaked and decocted with fresh ginger to reduce toxicity and enhance phlegm-transforming action; (b) bile-processing [dan zhi 膽制] — mixed with ox bile and dried to produce Dan Nan Xing (膽南星), shifting the nature from warm to cool and emphasizing anti-convulsive, heat-clearing actions. Classical texts caution against use in pregnancy [duo tai 墮胎 action noted] and in conditions of yin deficiency or dryness.

entities_mentioned.name: Tian Nan Xing 天南星
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arisaema erubescens (Wall.) Schott, Arisaema heterophyllum Blume, or Arisaema amurense Maxim. (family Araceae); dried rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: All three Arisaema species are officially recognized as sources of Tian Nan Xing in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia. The rhizomes contain toxic calcium oxalate raphides and irritant lectins, explaining the classical toxicity designation. The bile-processed form (Dan Nan Xing 膽南星) is a distinct pharmacological product with altered properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Dan Nan Xing 膽南星
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bile-processed Arisaema rhizome; preparation product, not a separate species
entities_mentioned.notes: Produced by mixing powdered Tian Nan Xing with fresh ox, sheep, or pig bile, then fermenting and drying. Shifts therapeutic character from warm-toxic to cool; used for phlegm-heat and pediatric convulsions in both Chinese medicine and Kampo.

entities_mentioned.name: Ban Xia 半夏
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino rhizome (Araceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently compared to Tian Nan Xing in classical texts; both are acrid, warm, toxic Araceae rhizomes that dry dampness and transform phlegm, but Tian Nan Xing is considered stronger for wind-phlegm and channel obstruction, while Ban Xia is preferred for Stomach-related phlegm and vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: Dao Tan Tang 導痰湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula (decoction)
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in Er Chen Tang variant tradition; key formula in both Chinese internal medicine and Kampo for phlegm-obstructed channels with dizziness, heaviness, and numbness.

entities_mentioned.name: Qian Zheng San 牽正散
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula (powder)
entities_mentioned.notes: From Yanshi Jiaocang Fang (楊氏家藏方, Song dynasty). Canonical formula for wind-phlegm facial paralysis; used in modern Kampo practice for Bell's palsy-type presentations.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a brief label rather than a full classical passage; accordingly, this entry reconstructs a comprehensive Plantacopia record from the Shennong Bencaojing inferior-herb tradition, Tang-Song materia medica elaborations (especially Bencao Yanyi 本草衍義 and Bencao Gangmu 本草綱目), and established Kampo (日本漢方) secondary literature. Where specific classical Chinese phrasing has been reconstructed to represent the tradition rather than quote a unique manuscript, this is indicated by context. The warning regarding pregnancy (duo tai 墮胎) is attested across multiple classical sources and is clinically significant. Practitioners should note that unprocessed Tian Nan Xing is a Schedule-controlled substance in several jurisdictions due to its acute oral toxicity.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Tian Nan Xing 天南星

results.title_inferred: White Mustard Seed (Bai Jie Zi): Classical Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 白芥子 (Bái Jiè Zǐ) — 白芥子，味辛，性溫。入肺經。主利氣豁痰，溫中散寒，通絡止痛，消腫散結。治寒痰壅肺，咳嗽氣逆，胸脅脹滿，痰飲流注關節，陰疽腫毒。
results.translation:
White Mustard Seed [Bái Jiè Zǐ, 白芥子]: Flavor: Acrid [xīn 辛]. Nature: Warm [wēn 溫]. Channel Tropism [guī jīng 歸經]: Primarily the Lung [Fèi 肺]; secondary association with the Stomach [Wèi 胃]. Classical Category: Middle Herb [zhōng pǐn 中品] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, though it receives fuller codification in later Tang and Song dynasty materia medica (notably the Bencao Yanyi and Bencao Gangmu) and in subsequent Kampo elaboration.

Therapeutic Actions: (1) Moves Qi [lì qì 利氣] and transforms Phlegm [huó tán 豁痰], particularly Cold-Phlegm [hán tán 寒痰] and Damp-Phlegm [shī tán 濕痰] lodged in the Lung; (2) Warms the Middle Burner [wēn zhōng 溫中] and disperses Cold [sàn hán 散寒]; (3) Unblocks the Collaterals [tōng luò 通絡] and alleviates pain [zhǐ tòng 止痛], especially bi-syndrome [bì zhèng 痺證] caused by Cold-Phlegm obstructing the joints and sinews; (4) Reduces swelling [xiāo zhǒng 消腫] and dissipates nodular accumulations [sàn jié 散結], including yin-type abscesses [yīn jū 陰疽] and subcutaneous phlegm nodules [tán hé 痰核].

Principal Indications: Cold-Phlegm congesting the Lung with cough and counterflow of Qi; fullness and distension of the chest and hypochondrium due to Phlegm-Fluid [tán yǐn 痰飲] obstruction; Phlegm-Fluid seeping into the joints [liú zhù 流注] causing swelling and fixed pain; yin-type toxic swellings that are pale, non-hot, and slow to suppurate.

Preparation Methods: The seed is most commonly stir-fried [chǎo 炒] to moderate its acridity, reduce skin irritation, and enhance its Phlegm-dissolving action. Raw seed [shēng yòng 生用] is used topically as a poultice [bú fǎ 敷法] applied to acupoints or affected joints (a technique prominent in Kampo and in San Fu Tian [三伏天] seasonal acupoint therapy) to draw out Cold-Phlegm and relieve joint obstruction. Ground into powder, it may be blended with ginger juice [jiāng zhī 薑汁] for external plasters.

Key Classical and Kampo Formulas: (1) Sān Zǐ Yǎng Qīn Tāng [三子養親湯, 'Three-Seed Decoction to Nourish Parents'] — paired with Zǐ Sū Zǐ (Perilla seed) and Lái Fú Zǐ (radish seed) for elderly patients with Cold-Phlegm, cough, dyspnea, and food stagnation; (2) Bái Jiè Zǐ Sǎn [白芥子散] — used in various Song-dynasty formulations for subcutaneous Phlegm nodules and scrofula; (3) Tiān Tái Wū Yào Sǎn variant applications and Kampo Keishi-bukuryo-gan [桂枝茯苓丸] adjunct use in cold-type bi-syndrome with joint swelling. In Kampo practice, Bai Jie Zi (Japanese: Hakkaishi [白芥子]) is specifically valued in formulas targeting cold-damp joint pain and pleural fluid retention [xuán yǐn 懸飲].

Japanese Kampo Name: Hakkaishi [白芥子 (はくがいし)]. Kampo elaborations, particularly from the Edo period (e.g., Todo Yoshimasu's school), emphasize its use in resolving 'hidden Phlegm' [fú tán 伏痰] in the musculoskeletal system, which aligns with its topical application on acupoints BL 13 (Feishu) and ST 40 (Fenglong) during San Fu Tian treatments for chronic respiratory disease — a practice that bridges classical Chinese and Japanese Kampo traditions.

Cautions: Contraindicated in Lung conditions due to Yin deficiency with Empty-Heat [yīn xū huǒ wàng 陰虛火旺]; prolonged topical application may cause blistering due to its strongly irritant volatile compounds (sinigrin/sinalbin hydrolysis products). Use with caution in patients with gastric sensitivity.

entities_mentioned.name: Bai Jie Zi 白芥子
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sinapis alba L. (White mustard; Brassicaceae family) — seeds of the white mustard plant. Distinct from Jie Zi (芥子, Brassica juncea), the brown/yellow mustard, though both are used medicinally.
entities_mentioned.notes: Active compounds include sinalbin (a glucosinolate), sinapine, and fixed oils. Hydrolysis of sinalbin yields p-hydroxybenzyl isothiocyanate, responsible for topical irritant and counter-irritant effects. The acrid, warming nature is pharmacologically consistent with its isothiocyanate content.

entities_mentioned.name: San Zi Yang Qin Tang 三子養親湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical TCM formula; no single-plant identification applicable.
entities_mentioned.notes: Attributed to Han Mao (韓懋) of the Ming dynasty (Hanshi Yi Tong, c. 1522 CE). Widely used in both TCM and Kampo for productive cough with Cold-Phlegm in the elderly. The formula's name ('Three Seeds to Nourish Parents') reflects its gentle yet effective profile suitable for aged patients.

entities_mentioned.name: Hakkaishi 白芥子 (Japanese Kampo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sinapis alba L. seeds — same botanical source as Chinese Bai Jie Zi.
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia. Kampo use closely mirrors TCM indications but places particular emphasis on musculoskeletal Cold-Phlegm and pleural fluid conditions. Edo-period Kampo physicians, especially those of the Koho-ha (古方派, Classical Formula School), documented its use in resolving stubborn Phlegm-Cold accumulations.

entities_mentioned.name: San Fu Tian 三伏天 Acupoint Therapy
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seasonal acupoint application therapy (tiān jiǔ 天灸); not a plant.
entities_mentioned.notes: A practice where paste made from Bai Jie Zi (and other acrid, warming herbs) is applied to specific acupoints during the three hottest periods of the lunar calendar to prevent and treat Cold-type respiratory and joint diseases. Bridges external and internal use of the herb.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a brief modern label ('White mustard — cold phlegm, joint') rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage. The original_text field above therefore presents a reconstructed representative classical-style Chinese description consistent with the Shennong Bencaojing tradition and its elaborations, as instructed for comprehensive Plantacopia entry generation. The translation and entry are synthesized from: (1) Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE) foundational categories; (2) Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1578 CE), which provides the most detailed classical account of Bai Jie Zi; (3) Kampo sources including the Edo-period Koho-ha tradition; and (4) the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (current edition) for Hakkaishi. Where the Shennong Bencaojing does not explicitly list Bai Jie Zi (it receives fuller treatment in later texts), this is flagged in the classical category field. No genuinely illegible passages are present; the source was a brief modern descriptor rather than OCR of a manuscript.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Bai Jie Zi 白芥子

results.title_inferred: Jie Geng (Platycodon Root): Classical Chinese and Kampo Monograph
results.original_text: 桔梗 — 味辛，微溫。主治胸脅痛如刀刺，腹滿腸鳴幽幽，驚恐悸氣。（《神農本草經》中品）
results.translation:
JIE GENG (桔梗, jiégěng) — Platycodon Root

**Chinese Name:** Jiégěng (桔梗)
**Japanese Kampō Name:** Kikyō (桔梗, ききょう)
**Classical Category:** Middle-grade herb [中品, zhōngpǐn] — the Shennong Bencaojing middle class denotes herbs capable of both nourishing the body and treating disease, used with some caution regarding dosage and duration.
**Modern Identification:** Platycodon grandiflorum (Jacq.) A.DC. (Campanulaceae); the dried root [根, gēn] is the medicinal part used.

---

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:**
Acrid [辛, xīn] and bitter [苦, kǔ]. The Shennong Bencaojing records it primarily as acrid; later commentators (notably Tao Hongjing, c. 500 CE, and the Bencao Gangmu) add bitter as a co-flavor, which accounts for its dispersing and descending actions simultaneously.

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:**
Neutral to slightly warm [微溫, wēi wēn]. Some Song-dynasty sources classify it as neutral [平, píng]; the slight warmth facilitates its movement through the chest and upper burner [上焦, shàng jiāo].

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:**
Primarily enters the Lung channel [肺經, Fèi jīng]; secondarily the Stomach channel [胃經, Wèi jīng]. Later Kampo systematizers (Yoshimasu Tōdō, 18th c.) emphasize its strong affinity for the throat and upper respiratory tract as an extension of its Lung-channel action.

---

**Therapeutic Actions — Shennong Bencaojing Tradition (c. 200 CE):**
1. Relieves chest and hypochondriac pain as if pierced by a knife [胸脅痛如刀刺, xiōng xié tòng rú dāo cì] — indicating its ability to move and diffuse stagnant qi in the chest.
2. Resolves abdominal fullness with gurgling intestinal sounds [腹滿腸鳴幽幽, fù mǎn cháng míng yōuyōu] — pointing to its action on downward-bearing turbid qi.
3. Calms fright, fear, and palpitations [驚恐悸氣, jīng kǒng jì qì] — an older indication less emphasized in later practice, possibly reflecting its settling of chaotic qi ascending to disturb the Heart.

**Expanded Therapeutic Actions — Subsequent Classical and Kampo Elaboration:**
1. **Opens and diffuses Lung qi [宣肺, xuān fèi]:** The herb's acrid nature disperses constraint in the Lung; used for cough, wheezing, and sensation of chest oppression whether from Wind-Cold or Wind-Heat invasion.
2. **Expels phlegm [祛痰, qū tán]:** One of the principal phlegm-transforming agents for the upper burner; effective for both Cold-phlegm (white, copious) and, combined with cooling herbs, for Hot-phlegm (yellow, thick).
3. **Benefits the throat and reduces swelling [利咽消腫, lì yān xiāo zhǒng]:** Particularly valued for sore throat, loss of voice, and throat obstruction. This became its signature indication in Kampo practice.
4. **Drains pus and treats abscess [排膿消癰, pái nóng xiāo yōng]:** Classical texts describe its use in Lung abscess [肺癰, fèi yōng] — presenting with cough producing foul-smelling, purulent sputum — and in throat abscess [喉癰].
5. **Acts as a 'boat' or 'guide' [舟楫之劑, zhōu jí zhī jì]:** A classical metaphor (attributed to Li Dongyuan, Jin-Yuan dynasty) describing Jie Geng's function as a 'messenger herb' that floats other medicinals upward to the chest and Lung when combined with them.
6. **Raises and lifts sunken Lung qi:** In combination with other herbs, assists in raising clear yang [清陽, qīng yáng]; used in formulas for prolapse, fatigue, and sinking sensations when there is concurrent Lung qi deficiency.

---

**Preparation Methods:**
- **Dried raw root [生桔梗, shēng jiégěng]:** Standard decoction [煎, jiān], typically 3–10 g. Raw form emphasized for throat and phlegm conditions.
- **Honey-prepared [蜜炙, mì zhì]:** Roasting with honey moderates its dispersing action and adds a moistening quality; preferred when treating dry cough or Lung yin deficiency with phlegm.
- **Powdered [散, sǎn]:** Used in classical formula preparations such as Jie Geng Tang where fine-ground powder is decocted briefly.
- **Contraindication note:** Classically contraindicated (or used cautiously) in cases of coughing up blood [咯血, kéxuè], as its lifting and diffusing action may aggravate upward-surging blood; also used with caution in Yin deficiency fire patterns.

---

**Key Classical Formulas:**
1. **Jie Geng Tang (桔梗湯)** — *Shang Han Lun* (Zhang Zhongjing, c. 220 CE): Jie Geng + Gan Cao (Licorice). The simplest and most celebrated formula for sore throat; this two-herb decoction directly addresses throat pain and swelling, and is the foundational Kampo formula (kikyō-tō) for pharyngitis and tonsillitis.
2. **Yin Qiao San (銀翹散)** — *Wen Bing Tiao Bian* (Wu Jutong, 1798): Contains Jie Geng to open the Lung and carry the formula's cooling herbs upward to the throat and chest; canonical formula for Wind-Heat invasion.
3. **Sang Ju Yin (桑菊飲)** — *Wen Bing Tiao Bian*: Uses Jie Geng to diffuse Lung qi and stop cough in early-stage Wind-Heat with cough.
4. **Shen Tong Zhu Yu Tang (身痛逐瘀湯)** and **Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang (血府逐瘀湯)** — both by Wang Qingren (c. 1830): Jie Geng included as the 'boat' to guide blood-moving herbs to the chest region.
5. **Pai Nong San (排膿散) / Pai Nong Tang (排膿湯)** — *Jin Gui Yao Lüe* (Zhang Zhongjing): Jie Geng as principal herb for draining pus in internal abscess, particularly Lung abscess.
6. **Kampo: Kikyō-sekko-tō (桔梗石膏湯)** — Jie Geng + Shi Gao (Gypsum): Japanese elaboration for acute purulent tonsillitis and throat abscess with heat signs; a Kampo-specific combination not found in the Chinese classical canon in this exact form.
7. **Kampo: Sho-seiryu-to (小青龍湯, Xiao Qing Long Tang)** — includes Jie Geng in some Kampo variant preparations for Cold-type wheezing with thin watery phlegm.

---

**Kampo (日本漢方) Elaborations:**
Kampo practice (particularly the Koho-ha [古方派] school of Yoshimasu Tōdō, 1702–1773) distilled Jie Geng's indications to a precise clinical core: **throat pain, purulent expectoration, and chest oppression**. Tōdō's *Yakuchō* (薬徴) states that Jie Geng's primary evidence [主治, shuchi] is 'pus in the chest and diaphragm area' [胸膈の膿], and that its throat-benefiting action is secondary to this fundamental pus-draining function. Modern Kampo granule preparations (エキス製剤) of Kikyō-tō remain a first-line Japanese over-the-counter treatment for pharyngitis and tonsillitis.

entities_mentioned.name: Jie Geng (桔梗)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Platycodon grandiflorum (Jacq.) A.DC., family Campanulaceae; dried root
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most unambiguous identifications in the materia medica tradition. Consistently identified as Platycodon grandiflorum across Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pharmacopeias. The Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP) officially lists Kikyo-kon (桔梗根) as the dried root of this species.

entities_mentioned.name: Gan Cao (甘草) — Licorice
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or G. glabra L. (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-herb in the two-ingredient Jie Geng Tang; mentioned only in the context of classical formula citation.

entities_mentioned.name: Shi Gao (石膏) — Gypsum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calcium sulfate dihydrate (CaSO₄·2H₂O), a mineral medicinal
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Jie Geng in the Kampo formula Kikyō-sekko-tō for purulent throat conditions with heat.

entities_mentioned.name: Lung abscess (肺癰, fèi yōng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely corresponds to pulmonary abscess or severe purulent pneumonia in modern terms
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical description of foul-smelling purulent sputum coughed up is consistent with modern lung abscess or bronchiectasis with secondary infection. Classical Pai Nong formulas were the primary treatment.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: The provided segment is a modern-language prompt/summary rather than a verbatim classical Chinese text. The 'original_text' field therefore reconstructs the most likely classical Chinese source passage (from the Shennong Bencaojing, Zhongpin chapter) based on standard recensions. The fuller monograph content in 'translation' is synthesized from the Shennong Bencaojing, Bencao Gangmu (Li Shizhen, 1596), Shang Han Lun, Jin Gui Yao Lüe, Wen Bing Tiao Bian, and Yoshimasu Tōdō's Yakuchō — all standard sources for this herb in the tradition requested. Scholars should consult the Mori Tateyuki recension of the Shennong Bencaojing (1854) and the Ren Yingqiu critical edition (1982) for textual variants.
2. CLASSIFICATION DISPUTE: Some recensions place Jie Geng in the lower-grade [下品, xiàpǐn] category rather than middle-grade; the middle-grade classification follows the majority-consensus recension and aligns with Tao Hongjing's Bencao Jing Jizhu.
3. FLAVOR DISPUTE: The original Shennong Bencaojing records only 辛 (acrid/pungent); the addition of bitter [苦] is a Han-to-Tang dynasty elaboration based on clinical observation of its descending/draining actions. Both are retained here as the tradition is composite.
4. 'BOAT HERB' METAPHOR: The 舟楫之劑 metaphor is Li Dongyuan's (李東垣, 1180–1251) formulation and is not in the Shennong Bencaojing itself; it is flagged here as a Jin-Yuan dynasty addition to the tradition.
5. MODERN PHARMACOLOGY (for context, not part of classical translation): Platycodon root contains platycodin saponins (桔梗皂苷, jiégěng zàogān) demonstrated to have expectorant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties in vitro and in animal models, lending partial scientific support to the classical phlegm-expelling and abscess-draining indications.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Jie Geng 桔梗

results.title_inferred: Xing Ren (Apricot Kernel): Lung Qi, Cough, and Constipation
results.original_text: 杏仁 — 苦，溫，有小毒。主咳逆上氣，雷鳴，喉痹，下氣，產乳，金創，寒心，賁豚。
results.translation:
**Xing Ren (杏仁, Apricot Kernel)**

**Chinese Name:** Xìng Rén (杏仁)
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Kyōnin (杏仁)
**Classical Category:** Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — possessing medicinal potency with minor toxicity; suitable for therapeutic use with appropriate caution.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Bitter [苦, kǔ]
**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Warm [溫, wēn]
**Toxicity:** Minor toxicity [有小毒, yǒu xiǎo dú]
**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Lung [肺], Large Intestine [大腸]

**Primary Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE):**
Xing Ren governs [主] the following conditions: coughing with qi counterflow and ascent [咳逆上氣]; rumbling sounds in the chest and abdomen [雷鳴, 'thunder-rumbling']; throat obstruction [喉痹, laryngeal impediment]; descending of qi [下氣, i.e., redirecting rebellious upward-flowing qi downward]; difficulties associated with childbirth and lactation [產乳]; wounds from metal implements [金創, i.e., traumatic lacerations]; cold sensation around the heart [寒心]; and running piglet disorder [賁豚, bēn tún — a classical pattern of surging qi from the lower abdomen toward the chest, associated with fear and kidney qi disorder].

**Kampo Elaboration (Japanese tradition):**
In subsequent Kampo practice, Xing Ren / Kyōnin was refined and expanded in its indications. It is principally employed to: (1) **Descend lung qi and relieve cough and wheezing** [降氣止咳平喘] — indicated for cough, dyspnea, and wheezing of both cold and heat patterns, though especially suited to externally-contracted wind-cold with lung qi obstruction; (2) **Moisten the intestines and unblock the bowels** [潤腸通便] — used for constipation due to intestinal dryness, particularly in the elderly, postpartum women, or constitutionally fluid-deficient patients. Kampo physicians emphasized the herb's dual action on the Lung-Large Intestine axis, reflecting the classical interior-exterior organ pairing [臟腑相表裏].

**Preparation Methods:**
Classically administered as a decoction [煎劑, jiān jì]. The outer skin and tip [皮尖] are traditionally removed before use to reduce irritant properties. When used for constipation, it may be ground into paste or incorporated into honey-based preparations. Prolonged or high-dose use is cautioned against due to minor toxicity (amygdalin content, which hydrolyzes to hydrogen cyanide).

**Key Classical Formulas Featuring Xing Ren:**
- **Ma Huang Tang [麻黃湯]** (Shang Han Lun, c. 220 CE): Ephedra, Xing Ren, Gui Zhi, Zhi Gan Cao — for wind-cold exterior excess with lung qi obstruction, cough, and wheezing.
- **Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang [麻杏石甘湯]** (Shang Han Lun): Ephedra, Xing Ren, Shi Gao, Zhi Gan Cao — for lung heat with cough and wheezing; widely used in Kampo for pediatric respiratory conditions.
- **Xing Su San [杏蘇散]** (Wen Bing Tiao Bian, later elaboration): For cool-dryness attacking the lung.
- **Run Chang Wan [潤腸丸]** (later formula tradition): For dry intestine constipation.
- **Ting Li Da Zao Xie Fei Tang [葶藶大棗瀉肺湯]** variants in Kampo sometimes incorporate Xing Ren for phlegm-heat congesting the lung.

entities_mentioned.name: Xing Ren / 杏仁
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus armeniaca L. (Rosaceae) — dried ripe seed kernel of the apricot; or Prunus mandshurica / Prunus sibirica in northern Chinese variants. Pharmacologically contains amygdalin (a cyanogenic glycoside), fatty oils, and proteins.
entities_mentioned.notes: Two pharmacopeial varieties exist in modern Chinese medicine: Ku Xing Ren [苦杏仁, bitter apricot kernel] from Prunus armeniaca var. ansu and related species, and Tian Xing Ren [甜杏仁, sweet apricot kernel] from Prunus dulcis. The Shennong Bencaojing entry and classical formulas refer to the bitter variety. The minor toxicity noted in the classical text corresponds to the amygdalin content, which releases hydrogen cyanide upon enzymatic hydrolysis — a well-documented toxicological mechanism now understood biochemically.

entities_mentioned.name: 賁豚 / Bēn Tún (Running Piglet Disorder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A classical Chinese medical syndrome characterized by a subjective sensation of qi surging from the lower abdomen (or flanks) upward toward the chest and throat, often accompanied by palpitations, anxiety, and dyspnea. No precise modern pathological equivalent, though it has been compared to panic disorder, paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, or functional gastrointestinal disorders.
entities_mentioned.notes: The inclusion of Bēn Tún in Xing Ren's indications in the Shennong Bencaojing is notable and reflects the early classical understanding of this herb's qi-descending action in a broad sense. Later Kampo and TCM traditions largely dropped this indication in favor of the more empirically refined lung and large intestine actions.

entities_mentioned.name: Ma Huang Tang / 麻黃湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula from Zhang Zhongjing's Shang Han Lun (Treatise on Cold Damage, c. 220 CE). One of the most fundamental Kampo prescriptions, still in clinical use in Japan.
entities_mentioned.notes: Xing Ren's role in Ma Huang Tang is to assist Ma Huang (Ephedra) in descending lung qi and relieving cough/wheezing, while also preventing the overly dispersing action of Ephedra from scattering qi too forcefully.

entities_mentioned.name: 喉痹 / Hóu Bì (Throat Obstruction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A classical term broadly covering pharyngeal and laryngeal inflammatory or obstructive conditions — likely encompassing acute pharyngitis, laryngitis, and peritonsillar conditions in modern terms.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided is a modern summary prompt rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field has been reconstructed from the canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for Xing Ren as preserved in the Zheng Lei Ben Cao and other scholarly editions. The reconstructed classical text reads: 杏仁，苦，溫，有小毒。主咳逆上氣，雷鳴，喉痹，下氣，產乳，金創，寒心，賁豚. This is the standard received text and is flagged here for transparency.
2. The 'running piglet' (賁豚) indication in the classical text is authentic to the Shennong Bencaojing tradition but is rarely emphasized in modern TCM or Kampo clinical practice for this herb; it is retained here for historical fidelity.
3. Toxicity warning: Bitter Xing Ren contains amygdalin at concentrations sufficient to cause cyanide poisoning in overdose, particularly in children. The classical notation 有小毒 ('minor toxicity') is a meaningful clinical caution, not merely conventional formulaic language.
4. The channel tropism (Lung and Large Intestine) is not explicitly stated in the Shennong Bencaojing itself — this systematization developed in the Song-Jin-Yuan period and was fully codified by the Ming dynasty. It is included here as it is essential to Kampo and TCM clinical application and is standard in all modern Bencao scholarship.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Xing Ren 杏仁

results.title_inferred: Zhi Su Zi (Perilla Seed) — Lung Qi, Cough, and Constipation
results.original_text: 紫蘇子 (Zhǐ Sū Zǐ) — Perilla frutescens var. crispa, semen. Classical category: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn]. Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Acrid [辛, xīn]. Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Warm [溫, wēn]. Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Lung [肺, fèi], Large Intestine [大腸, dà cháng].
results.translation:
Zhi Su Zi (紫蘇子, 'Purple Perilla Seed') is the dried, ripe seed of Perilla frutescens (L.) Britton var. crispa (Thunb.) Hand.-Mazz. (family Lamiaceae). It is classified within the Middle Herb [中品] grade of the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, denoting a substance of moderate potency used to tonify, supplement, and treat illness, suitable for extended but not indefinite use.

**Five Flavors [五味]:** Acrid [辛, xīn].
**Four Natures [四氣]:** Warm [溫, wēn].
**Channel Tropism [歸經]:** Lung [肺 Fèi] and Large Intestine [大腸 Dà Cháng] channels.

**Primary Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition and subsequent elaboration):**
1. Descends Lung qi [降肺氣, jiàng fèi qì] and arrests cough and wheezing [止咳平喘, zhǐ ké píng chuǎn]: Zhi Su Zi is the principal seed-form herb for directing rebellious Lung qi downward. It is indicated where upward-counterflowing Lung qi produces chronic or acute cough, productive expectoration of phlegm, and wheezing or dyspnoea. The seed's oily, heavy, and descending character makes it especially suited to phlegm-damp or phlegm-cold obstructing the Lung.
2. Moistens the Intestines and unblocks the bowels [潤腸通便, rùn cháng tōng biàn]: The rich fatty-oil content of the seed moistens intestinal dryness, making Zhi Su Zi appropriate for constipation arising from dryness of the Large Intestine, particularly in the elderly, postpartum patients, or those with qi deficiency who lack the strength to move the stool.
3. Resolves phlegm [化痰, huà tán]: By descending qi and warming the Lung, the herb assists in the transformation and expectoration of cold or damp phlegm accumulations.

**Japanese Kampo Name:** 紫蘇子 (Shisoshi). In Kampo medicine the herb retains essentially the same indications as in the Chinese classical tradition, with particular emphasis on its use in elderly patients presenting with simultaneous wheezing and constipation — a pairing directly addressed by the formula Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang (see below).

**Key Classical Formulas:**
- **Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang [蘇子降氣湯]** (Tang-dynasty, Beiji Qianjin Yaofang tradition; also standard in Kampo as Soshikokito): The chief formula for descending Lung qi and dissolving phlegm-cold; indicated for cough, wheezing, fullness in the chest, and concurrent lower-body cold deficiency. Zhi Su Zi is the sovereign [君, jūn] herb.
- **San Zi Yang Qin Tang [三子養親湯]** (Ming dynasty, Han Shi Yi Tong): A three-seed formula (Zhi Su Zi, Bai Jie Zi [白芥子], Lai Fu Zi [萊菔子]) specifically designed for elderly patients with phlegm obstructing the Lung causing food stagnation, cough, and wheezing.
- **Su Zi Ma Ren Wan [蘇子麻仁丸]** and related moistening-laxative formulas employ Zhi Su Zi primarily for its bowel-lubricating action.

**Preparation Methods (classical):**
- Raw [生用, shēng yòng]: Stronger descending and bowel-moistening action.
- Stir-fried [炒用, chǎo yòng]: Slightly reduced in cold-moving properties; phlegm-resolving and qi-descending actions are considered more moderate and better tolerated in deficient patients. Stir-frying also improves fragrance and facilitates decoction extraction of active constituents.
- Typical decoction dosage in classical and modern practice: 3–9 g (up to 15 g in some Kampo protocols for elderly constipation).

**Cautions (classical):** Extended use in patients with Lung qi deficiency or Spleen deficiency with loose stools should be approached with caution, as the descending and moistening nature of the seed may further weaken the Spleen-Stomach dynamic [脾胃, pí wèi] and aggravate diarrhoea.

entities_mentioned.name: 紫蘇子 Zhi Su Zi (Zhǐ Sū Zǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Perilla frutescens (L.) Britton var. crispa (Thunb.) Hand.-Mazz., semen (family Lamiaceae) — dried ripe seed of purple perilla
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated across East and Southeast Asia. The seed is distinguished from the leaf (Zi Su Ye 紫蘇葉) and stem (Zi Su Geng 紫蘇梗), each of which carries distinct classical indications. Modern pharmacological studies identify rosmarinic acid, luteolin, apigenin, and a high proportion of alpha-linolenic acid in the seed oil as likely bioactive constituents corresponding to classical anti-inflammatory, antitussive, and laxative actions.

entities_mentioned.name: Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang 蘇子降氣湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula; standard preparation in both Chinese Medicine and Japanese Kampo (Soshikokito, TJ-114 in Tsumura granule numbering)
entities_mentioned.notes: Sovereign herb is Zhi Su Zi. Formula targets phlegm-cold obstructing the Lung with underlying Kidney yang deficiency — a pattern common in elderly patients with chronic obstructive presentations.

entities_mentioned.name: San Zi Yang Qin Tang 三子養親湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical three-herb formula from Han Shi Yi Tong (韓氏醫通), Ming dynasty (c. 1522 CE)
entities_mentioned.notes: Name translates approximately as 'Three-Seed Decoction to Nourish the Parents,' reflecting its design for elderly patients (whose care was the filial duty of adult children). The three seeds — Zhi Su Zi, Bai Jie Zi, Lai Fu Zi — each address a distinct aspect of phlegm-food-qi obstruction.

entities_mentioned.name: Bai Jie Zi 白芥子
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sinapis alba L. (white mustard seed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-herb in San Zi Yang Qin Tang; primarily warms and resolves cold phlegm lodged in the muscles and channels.

entities_mentioned.name: Lai Fu Zi 萊菔子
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Raphanus sativus L. (radish seed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-herb in San Zi Yang Qin Tang; descends qi and disperses food stagnation accompanying phlegm accumulation.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a modern scholarly digest rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the canonical technical descriptor in classical Chinese medical register rather than quoting a specific Shennong Bencaojing chapter, as the Bencaojing does not contain a discrete entry for Zhi Su Zi by that name (perilla seed enters the materia medica literature more prominently in the Tang and Song dynasties). The Middle Herb [中品] classification follows the Bencao Jing Ji Zhu (陶弘景, c. 500 CE) and Bencao Gangmu (李時珍, 1596 CE) rather than the earliest Shennong stratum. The Kampo elaborations (Soshikokito formula number, dosage norms) reflect standard Kampo clinical reference texts including the Yakuchū Shōkanron and current Japanese Ministry of Health Kampo extract granule monographs. No genuinely illegible passages were encountered; the segment was well-formed.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Zhi Su Zi 紫蘇子

results.title_inferred: Loquat Leaf (Pi Pa Ye): Classical Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 枇杷葉 — Pi Pa Ye — 肺熱、胃氣逆
results.translation:
Pi Pa Ye (枇杷葉, Loquat Leaf)

Chinese Name: Pi Pa Ye (枇杷葉)
Japanese Kampo Name: Biwa-yō (枇杷葉)

Classical Category: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — herbs that tonify deficiency and supplement weakness; used in moderation with attention to dosage.

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Bitter [苦, kǔ]
Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Cool [涼, liáng]
Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Lung [肺, fèi], Stomach [胃, wèi]

Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition and Kampo elaborations):

1. Clears Lung Heat and Descends Lung Qi [清肺熱、降肺氣]: Pi Pa Ye is principally indicated for cough arising from heat lodged in the Lung organ system. Its bitter flavor and cool nature direct heat downward and outward. It is especially suited to cough with thick or yellow phlegm, dry or irritated throat, and feverish sensation in the chest.

2. Redirects Rebellious Stomach Qi Downward [降胃氣、止嘔逆]: The Stomach governs descent; when Stomach Qi rebels upward, symptoms such as nausea, hiccup [呃逆, ě nì], and vomiting arise. Pi Pa Ye's descending and harmonizing action on the Stomach channel makes it a classical remedy for these presentations, particularly when accompanied by heat signs.

3. Transforms Phlegm [化痰, huà tán]: Bitter herbs dry dampness and transform phlegm. Pi Pa Ye assists in resolving phlegm-heat accumulation in the Lung, making it useful in presentations of productive cough with difficult expectoration.

4. Moistens and Soothes the Throat [潤喉利咽]: In Kampo elaborations, Pi Pa Ye is noted for a secondary moistening quality that soothes irritated mucous membranes of the throat and bronchi, complementing its heat-clearing action.

Preparation Notes:
— The leaf must be brushed free of the fine hairs [毛, máo] on its underside before use, as the hairs are considered irritating to the throat and may provoke cough if not removed. This is a consistent classical and Kampo processing instruction.
— Typically prepared as a decoction [煎劑, jiān jì]; honey-frying [蜜炙, mì zhì] is used in Kampo and later Chinese practice to enhance moistening and cough-suppressing properties.
— Standard dosage in classical decoction: 6–12 g (dry leaf, hairs removed).

Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:
1. Pi Pa Qing Fei Yin [枇杷清肺飲] — 'Loquat Lung-Clearing Beverage': addresses Lung heat with cough and skin eruptions (notably used in Kampo for acne associated with Lung heat).
2. Qing Zao Jiu Fei Tang [清燥救肺湯] — 'Dryness-Clearing Lung-Rescuing Decoction' (喻昌, Qing dynasty): Pi Pa Ye serves as a supporting herb to descend Lung Qi and clear heat-dryness injuring the Lung.
3. Bi Pa Ye Wan [枇杷葉丸] — referenced in Kampo compendium texts for chronic cough with phlegm-heat.

Classical Commentary:
The Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目] (Li Shizhen, 1596) affirms the use of Pi Pa Ye for 'all coughs and vomiting,' noting its action 'descends the qi of the five organs.' Kampo physicians of the Edo period (notably in the Kohō-ha [古方派] tradition) employed Pi Pa Ye primarily in heat-pattern respiratory and digestive complaints, valuing its reliable descending and cooling actions without being excessively cold or injurious to Spleen Yang.

entities_mentioned.name: Pi Pa Ye 枇杷葉
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eriobotrya japonica (Thunb.) Lindl. — Loquat; family Rosaceae. The dried leaf (folium Eriobotryae) is the medicinal part.
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated across East and Southeast Asia. The leaf's fine abaxial hairs (trichomes) are a consistent processing concern in all classical traditions. Modern pharmacological research has identified ursolic acid, oleanolic acid, and amygdalin as key constituents, with demonstrated expectorant and anti-inflammatory activity consistent with classical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Biwa-yō 枇杷葉 (Japanese Kampo name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eriobotrya japonica — same plant as Pi Pa Ye
entities_mentioned.notes: The loquat tree was introduced to Japan from China and became well established in Kampo pharmacy. The Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP) recognizes Biwa-yō as an official crude drug.

entities_mentioned.name: Pi Pa Qing Fei Yin 枇杷清肺飲
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula; source attributed to various Qing-period compilations. Regularly employed in modern Kampo for acneiform dermatitis associated with Lung heat pattern.

entities_mentioned.name: Qing Zao Jiu Fei Tang 清燥救肺湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Composed by Yù Chāng (喻昌) in the Qing dynasty; a major formula for dry-heat injuring the Lung. Pi Pa Ye functions as a secondary descending and heat-clearing agent within this compound formula.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a modern summary note rather than a verbatim classical passage; accordingly, this entry reconstructs the classical and Kampo doctrinal content from the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, Bencao Gangmu, and Kampo formulary literature rather than translating a specific ancient text. All classical attributions are consistent with the received tradition. The instruction to remove leaf hairs before decoction is among the most consistently repeated processing notes in Chinese materia medica and should be flagged for any practical preparation context. Channel tropism (Lung and Stomach) is not explicitly stated in the Shennong Bencaojing itself (channel theory was elaborated post-Han) but is the standard attribution in all subsequent systematic materia medica from the Tang period onward.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Pi Pa Ye 枇杷葉

results.title_inferred: Sang Bai Pi (Mulberry Root Bark): Lung Heat and Edema
results.original_text: 桑白皮，味甘，寒。主傷中，五勞六極，羸瘦，崩中，脈絕，補虛益氣。葉：除寒熱，出汗。桑耳黑者，主女子漏下赤白汁，血病，癥瘕積聚，陰補陰陽，寒熱，無子。五木耳名蕈，益氣不飢，輕身強志。生山谷。
results.translation:
Sang Bai Pi [Mulberry Root Bark, 桑白皮]; flavor: sweet [gan 甘]; nature: cold [han 寒]. It governs injury to the interior [shang zhong 傷中], the five labors and six exhaustions [wu lao liu ji 五勞六極], emaciation and wasting [lei shou 羸瘦], flooding discharge [beng zhong 崩中], interrupted pulse [mai jue 脈絕]; it supplements vacuity and augments qi [bu xu yi qi 補虛益氣]. The leaves [ye 葉]: eliminate cold and heat [han re 寒熱] and promote sweating [chu han 出汗]. Black mulberry ear fungus [sang er hei zhe 桑耳黑者] governs: red and white vaginal discharge [lou xia chi bai 漏下赤白], blood diseases [xue bing 血病], conglomerations, concretions, and accumulations [zheng jia ji ju 癥瘕積聚]; it supplements yin and yang [bu yin yang 補陰陽], addresses cold and heat [han re 寒熱], and treats infertility [wu zi 無子]. The five wood ears [wu mu er 五木耳], also called xun [蕈 fungus/mushroom], augment qi [yi qi 益氣], prevent hunger [bu ji 不飢], lighten the body [qing shen 輕身], and strengthen the will [qiang zhi 強志]. [This plant] grows in mountain valleys [sheng shan gu 生山谷].

— KAMPO ELABORATION —
Japanese Kampo tradition identifies this herb as Sōhakuhi (桑白皮, pronounced Sō-haku-hi). Its primary therapeutic domain in the Kampo framework is the Lung organ system, where it is used to drain Lung heat [haishi qingfei 瀉肺清熱] and descend rebellious Lung qi to relieve cough and wheeze [xuan fei ping chuan 宣肺平喘]. Secondary actions include promoting urination and dispersing edema [li shui xiao zhong 利水消腫]. It is used prominently in conditions now correlated with bronchitis, asthma, pleurisy with fluid accumulation, and nephritic or cardiac edema.

Key classical formulas featuring Sang Bai Pi:
1. Xie Bai San (瀉白散, 'Drain the White Powder,' from Qian Yi's Xiao Er Yao Zheng Zhi Jue, c.1119 CE): Sang Bai Pi + Di Gu Pi + Gan Cao + Jing Mi — treats Lung heat manifesting as chronic cough, steaming bone fever, and skin heat; considered the foundational formula for draining Lung heat.
2. Hua Gai San (華蓋散): Sang Bai Pi combined with Ma Huang, Xing Ren, Su Zi, Fu Ling, Chen Pi, Gan Cao — treats wind-cold obstructing the Lung with phlegm, wheezing, and cough.
3. Wu Pi San / Wu Pi Yin (五皮散/五皮飲): Sang Bai Pi + Chen Pi + Fu Ling Pi + Da Fu Pi + Sheng Jiang Pi — classic formula for generalized edema and abdominal distension due to Spleen-Lung water metabolism dysfunction.
4. Sang Bai Pi Tang (桑白皮湯, from Wan Bing Hui Chun): treats Lung heat phlegm-fire cough and wheezing.

Classical Category: Middle herb [zhong pin 中品] in the Shennong Bencaojing tripartite classification (superior/middle/inferior), though some editions list it under superior herbs; the middle classification is more commonly cited in scholarly editions.

Channel Tropism (Gui Jing 歸經): Lung [Fei 肺]; subsidiary tropism to Spleen [Pi 脾] and Small Intestine [Xiao Chang 小腸] in later elaborations.

Five Flavors (Wu Wei 五味): Sweet [Gan 甘].
Four Natures (Si Qi 四氣): Cold [Han 寒].

Preparation: Classically, the root bark is harvested in autumn or winter, the coarse outer bark is scraped away, and the inner white bark is dried. It may be used raw (sheng yong 生用) to more strongly drain heat and promote urination, or honey-fried (mi zhi 蜜炙) to moderate its cold nature and emphasize its qi-supplementing and cough-relieving properties. Standard decoction: 6–12 g.

entities_mentioned.name: Sang Bai Pi 桑白皮 / Sōhakuhi (桑白皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. — root bark of the white mulberry tree (family Moraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified herbs in the Chinese materia medica tradition. The root bark of Morus alba is pharmacologically documented to contain flavonoids (including mulberroside and cyclomulberrosin), prenylated flavones, alkaloids, and polyphenols. Modern studies confirm bronchodilatory, diuretic, anti-inflammatory, and hypoglycemic activities consistent with classical indications. The SHENNONG BENCAOJING entry is notable for also describing the mulberry leaf (sang ye 桑葉), black mulberry fungus (sang er 桑耳, likely Auricularia auricula or Phellinus igniarius), and general wood-ear fungi (mu er 木耳), reflecting the classical encyclopedic approach of cataloguing multiple parts and associated organisms of a single plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Sang Er 桑耳 (Black Mulberry Ear Fungus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Phellinus igniarius (L.) Quél. (Hymenochaetaceae) or Auricularia auricula-judae (Bull.) Quél. (Auriculariaceae) growing on mulberry trees
